_about: Per-term Definition + History for every term page (Overview tab shows the definition; Origin & History tab shows the history). Written to docs/templates/term_definition.md and term_history.md, grounded in the research dossiers (data/research/) + etymology.json + the databases. Verify quotes against sources before citing. Flagship terms also have a long-form Word History in data/essays.json.

wizard.definition: A wizard is a worker of *learned* magic — one whose power comes from study, books, and mastered lore rather than from a pact or an inborn gift. In the fantasy tradition the wizard is the scholarly magic-user par excellence, staff in one hand and grimoire in the other.
wizard.history: The word begins as an honorific: Middle English *wysard*, 'wise one' (from *wys*, 'wise,' + the agent-suffix *-ard*), meaning a sage or philosopher. Only in the 1550s does it harden toward the sinister — a man whose wisdom shades into forbidden art. Tolkien's Gandalf fixed the modern image in 1954, and Magic: The Gathering inherits it wholesale: *Wizard* is the game's largest magic-user type, 1,288 creatures strong, and skews blue — the color of knowledge.

sorcerer.definition: A sorcerer wields *sorcery* — magic figured as innate or fate-bending, worked by will and formula. Where a wizard studies, the sorcerer channels; the line is loose, but the sorcerer keeps a whiff of the illicit.
sorcerer.history: Buried in the word is an ancient practice: *sorcerer* comes through Old French *sorcier* from Vulgar Latin *sortiarius*, 'a caster of lots,' from Latin *sors, sortem*, 'lot, fate' — the same *sors* behind *sortilege*. A sorcerer was, at root, one who manipulates fortune by casting lots. The magical sense arrives in English around the 1520s. In Magic the word survives mostly as the card type *Sorcery*, a rules term now, far from its fortune-telling origin.

witch.definition: A witch is a practitioner of witchcraft — folk and ritual magic, healing or harming — historically and unjustly imagined as bound in pact to the Devil. One of the oldest magic-words in English, and among the most contested.
witch.history: Old English had *wicce* (feminine) and *wicca* (masculine), beside the verb *wiccian*, 'to work magic'; the deeper root is genuinely uncertain, perhaps tied to 'bending' or to 'sacred rite.' The word's darkest chapter is early-modern — the trials that killed tens of thousands, powered by manuals like the *Malleus Maleficarum* (1487). Modern historians dismantled the myth of a real witch-cult, and 20th-century Wicca reinvented the witch as a chosen, positive identity.

sources.label: Ronald Hutton, The Triumph of the Moon
sources.abstract: Traces how modern Wicca -- the pagan witchcraft religion that emerged in 20th-century England -- was actually assembled by blending older folklore, ceremonial magic, and Romantic ideas about nature, rather than being an unbroken survival of ancient paganism.

sources.label: Norman Cohn, Europe's Inner Demons
sources.abstract: Traces where the medieval stereotype of witches flying off to secret orgies with the devil actually came from, and shows that key 'evidence' used to justify witch hunts was based on invented stories and outright forgeries rather than real observed practices.

warlock.definition: A warlock is, in modern fantasy, a male magic-user — often one who draws power from a pact with a darker patron. By origin the word has nothing to do with being male: it means an oath-breaker.
warlock.history: Old English *wǣrloga* is 'oath-breaker, traitor' — *wǣr*, 'pledge,' + *-loga*, 'liar.' Applied first to the Devil himself, the great covenant-breaker, it drifted in Scots toward 'a man in league with the Devil,' and thence to the fantasy 'male witch.' The pact-with-a-patron flavor of the modern warlock is, remarkably, latent in the etymology: a warlock is defined by a broken or dark covenant.

mage.definition: A mage is a magic-user — the lean, lofty synonym of *wizard* and *magician*, carrying a faint hieratic dignity. It is the commonest literary word for a spellcaster in the game.
mage.history: *Mage* descends from Latin *magus*, from Greek *magos*, from Old Persian *maguš* — the Zoroastrian priestly caste of Persia, keepers of fire and star-lore. Their name is the root of *magic* itself, and of the biblical *magi*. The word enters English in the late 14th century. Magic uses *mage* more than almost any other magic-word in its flavor text (417 uses), and built a whole set, Strixhaven, around five colleges of them.

shaman.definition: A shaman is a spirit-mediating specialist — one who enters trance to travel among spirits, heal, and divine on a community's behalf. In fantasy the shaman is the primal magic-user, close to nature and the ancestors.
shaman.history: Unlike almost every other word here, *shaman* is not European: English took it in the 1690s from Russian *shamán*, from the Tungusic (Evenki) *šamán* of Siberia. Anthropology then stretched it to cover trance-specialists worldwide — a generalization scholars now handle with care. Magic makes *Shaman* a major creature type, skewing green and red, the colors of nature and primal fury.

druid.definition: A druid is a priest of the ancient Celts — and, in fantasy, a nature-priest who draws magic from the living world. The word carries the forest with it.
druid.history: *Druid* comes through Latin *druidēs* from Gaulish, on a Proto-Celtic root *dru-wid-*, 'oak-knower' or 'strong-seer' (*dru-*, oak/strong, + *weid-*, to see/know — the same *weid-* behind *wit* and *vision*). The historical druids left no writings; nearly everything romantic about them is later reconstruction. Magic's druids are green mana-priests, tending the mana of the land.

cleric.definition: A cleric is a divine spellcaster — one whose magic flows from a god or a sacred office rather than from arcane study. The word sits exactly on the border of religion and magic.
cleric.history: *Cleric* comes from Late Latin *clericus*, from Greek *klērikos*, from *klēros*, 'a lot, an inheritance': the clergy are 'God's lot,' his portion. That lot-metaphor is the very one hidden in *sorcerer* (a caster of lots) — pointed, here, in the opposite, holy direction. Dungeons & Dragons made the cleric a healing spellcaster class; Magic's Clerics skew white and black, the colors of order and sacrifice.

necromancer.definition: A necromancer commands or consults the dead — in the older sense a diviner who questions spirits for hidden knowledge, in the modern fantasy sense a sorcerer who raises corpses to serve. Magic means the latter.
necromancer.history: To a Greek, *nekromanteia* was corpse-*divination* — summoning the dead to ask them, as Odysseus does in the Odyssey — and it belonged with augury and scrying. The turn came in medieval Latin, which reshaped *necromantia* into *nigromantia*, as if from *niger*, 'black': death-divination was folk-etymologized into 'the black arts,' and its link to the dead loosened. The full story is in this term's Word History.

sources.label: Frank Klaassen, The Transformations of Magic
sources.abstract: This book argues that medieval magic banned by the church came in two flavors: "image magic" (making charms and talismans), which scholars could half-justify with the science of the day, and "ritual magic" (rituals for summoning spirits), which could not. Klaassen traces how interest in the first kind faded after 1500 while the second kept going and helped shape early modern magic.

summon.definition: To summon is to call a being — spirit, demon, or creature — into presence and service. A summoning brings something *here*; the drama is in what answers.
summon.history: Latin *summonēre* meant 'to remind privately, to hint' (*sub-* + *monēre*, 'to warn'); the legal *summons* and the magical *summoning* share that root sense of a formal calling-forth. The occult sense sharpens in the ritual-magic tradition, where to summon a spirit was to compel it to appear by name and seal. Magic once headed every creature spell 'Summon' (Summon Wizard); the wording is gone, but calling a creature into play is still the heart of the game.

hex.definition: A hex is a curse — a spell worked to harm — and, as a verb, to lay one. It is the homely, folk-magic word for malice worked at a distance.
hex.history: *Hex* is a young loan for an old idea: American English took it in the 1830s from Pennsylvania-German *Hexe*, 'witch,' from Old High German *hagazussa*, 'hedge-rider' — the witch who rides the hedge between the tame village and the wild beyond. So a hex is, at root, hedge-magic. Magic uses the word in curse-and-witch flavor and in *Hexproof* — protection from being targeted, that is, from being hexed.

curse.definition: A curse is a malediction — words or rites meant to bring harm, misfortune, or doom on a target. It is the oldest hostile magic, and among the best-attested in the archaeological record.
curse.history: *Curse* is an etymological dead end: Old English *curs*, of genuinely unknown origin, with no secure cognate in any related language — a curse-word whose own past is, fittingly, obscure. The practice is ancient and vivid: Greek and Roman *defixiones*, lead tablets inscribed with binding curses, survive by the thousand. Magic makes 'Curse' an Aura subtype — an enchantment you fix onto an opponent.

sources.label: John Gager, Curse Tablets and Binding Spells
sources.abstract: Gager collects and translates ancient Greek and Roman "curse tablets" -- thin sheets of lead or other metal inscribed with spells asking gods or spirits to harm a rival, a thief, or a romantic competitor -- with commentary explaining how ordinary people, not just professional sorcerers, used these rituals in everyday disputes.

enchant.definition: To enchant is to bind someone or something under a spell — and, softened, to charm or delight. An *enchantment* is the spell so laid, or the lasting magical effect it leaves.
enchant.history: The word is literally a song: Latin *incantare*, 'to sing a spell upon' (*in-* + *cantare*, 'to sing') — the same *cantare* behind *chant*, *incantation*, and *cantor*. Its learned twin, *incant*, is the very same Latin word borrowed twice, once early through French and once late from books. In Magic, *Enchant* is the defining keyword of the Aura — the spell you sing onto a permanent — and 'enchantment' is a core card type.

spell.definition: A spell is a formula of words that works magic — the utterance that does something. In the game it is the most basic unit of action: a card you cast.
spell.history: Old English *spell* meant simply 'a saying, a story, a sermon' — *gospel* is *gōd-spell*, 'good news,' and spelling out letters is the same word. The 'magic formula' sense is the youngest of the three, sharpening around 1580. Magic made *spell* its most fundamental term, which is why it shows up in over 8,000 rules texts but barely a hundred flavor texts: in Magic, 'spell' is mechanics, not story.

ward.definition: A ward is a protective magical guard — a boundary or charm that keeps harm out. To ward is to defend by such means.
ward.history: *Ward* is Old English *weardian*, 'to guard, watch' (Proto-Germanic *wardōn*); French borrowed the same Germanic root back as *guard*, making the two a doublet split by the Channel. A magical ward is protective magic in its oldest, plainest form. Magic made *Ward* an evergreen keyword in 2020 — a tax an opponent must pay to target the warded permanent — the ancient shielding charm rendered as a rule.

divine.definition: To divine is to discover the hidden — especially the future — by supernatural means. *Divination* is the whole art; a *diviner* practices it. The word claims a godlike power of sight.
divine.history: Latin *divinare*, 'to foresee,' from *divinus*, 'of a god': to divine is to do what the gods do, know what is hidden. The Roman world formalized it into a state science — augury, haruspicy, the reading of oracles — that Cicero could both catalogue and doubt in his *De Divinatione*. Water-diviners kept the word literal into modern times; Magic names a card-drawing spell 'Divination' — foresight as card advantage.

sources.label: Cicero, De Divinatione
sources.abstract: In this ancient dialogue, the Roman statesman Cicero has two speakers argue over whether divination -- predicting the future through omens, dreams, and oracles -- is a genuine skill or just wishful superstition, ultimately siding with reason and skepticism over popular belief.

augur.definition: An augur was a Roman priest who read the will of the gods in the flight and feeding of birds; *augury* is the art. To augur is to foretell from signs.
augur.history: Latin *augur* named the official who took the *auspices* (*avis*, 'bird,' + *specere*, 'to look') before any public act; the word may link to *augēre*, 'to increase, to bless.' The ritual survives fossilized in *inauguration* — we still 'inaugurate' by taking good omens. Magic keeps *augury* as flavor for its blue and white seers, and made 'Augur' a recurring card name.

oracle.definition: An oracle is a mouthpiece of prophecy — a shrine, a priestess, or the divine utterance itself. The word names both the speaker and the thing spoken.
oracle.history: Latin *oraculum*, from *orare*, 'to speak, to pray' — an oracle is a 'speaking-place,' the god's mouth (the same *orare* behind *orator* and *oration*). Greece's oracles, above all Delphi, were institutions of state, and the Sibyls their freelance cousins. Magic's Theros set (2013) drew directly on this world, and 'Oracle' recurs as a title for its blue seers.

sources.label: H. W. Parke, Sibyls and Sibylline Prophecy in Classical Antiquity
sources.abstract: Parke traces the legendary Sibyls -- prophetic women in the ancient Mediterranean believed to deliver cryptic verse prophecies -- from their earliest appearances in Greek sources through their adoption by the Romans and, later, early Christians who reinterpreted their sayings as predictions of Christ.

omen.definition: An omen is a portent — a sign read as foretelling good or ill. Reading omens is among the oldest of the divinatory arts.
omen.history: Latin *omen*, 'a foreboding sign,' of obscure ultimate root. Its most curious descendant is *abominable*, long (wrongly) parsed as *ab-omen*, 'away from a good omen' — a folk-etymology strong enough to have shaped the spelling. Mesopotamian scribes compiled vast omen-lists three thousand years before Rome; Magic uses 'Omen' in card names and, in Eldraine, as an adventure-style mechanic.

sources.label: Jean Bottéro, Mesopotamia
sources.abstract: This is a collection of essays by French scholar Jean Bottéro (published as 'Mesopotamia: Writing, Reasoning, and the Gods') about the ancient civilizations of Mesopotamia, the region between the Tigris and Euphrates rivers in what is now Iraq. It covers how writing was invented there and how people practiced divination — reading signs like the shapes of animal organs to learn the will of the gods — arguing this careful, rule-based thinking was an early ancestor of scientific reasoning.

astrology.definition: Astrology is divination by the stars and planets — reading celestial positions for their influence on earthly life. For most of history it was inseparable from astronomy.
astrology.history: Greek *astron*, 'star,' + *-logia*, 'study.' From Babylonian omen-astronomy through Ptolemy's *Tetrabiblos* (2nd c.) and the great Arabic astrologers, astrology was a rigorous technical science — it chose the hour to make a talisman or crown a king. Only in the 17th century did astrology and astronomy divorce; Kepler cast horoscopes to the end. RenaissanceMagicDB catalogues its apparatus of aspects, dignities, and houses.

sources.label: Tamsyn Barton, Ancient Astrology
sources.abstract: This book explains how astrology (the belief that the positions of stars and planets influence human events) developed in Mesopotamia and spread through Greece and Rome, and examines the social role astrologers played and why the practice appealed to people at every level of ancient society.

elixir.definition: An elixir is a perfecting or life-giving preparation — in alchemy the very agent of transmutation, the philosophers' stone in liquid form, and by extension the draught of health or immortality.
elixir.history: *Elixir* comes through Medieval Latin from Arabic *al-iksīr*, 'the philosophers' stone,' itself likely from Greek *xērion*, a 'drying powder' for wounds — so the word climbs from a medical powder to the transmuting stone to the water of eternal life. That *al-* is the Arabic article, as in *alchemy* and *alcohol*. Magic uses 'Elixir' for life-giving artifacts.

sources.label: Lyndy Abraham, A Dictionary of Alchemical Imagery
sources.abstract: A reference book that decodes the strange symbols alchemists used, like the "green lion" or the "philosopher's stone," explaining both their literal meaning as steps in a chemical process and the spiritual meaning that writers and poets later gave them, illustrated with historical woodcuts and engravings.

transmute.definition: To transmute is to change one substance into another — the central operation of alchemy, moving matter across the boundary of its own nature: lead into gold, body into spirit.
transmute.history: Latin *transmutare* (*trans-*, 'across,' + *mutare*, 'to change') — the dream-verb of the whole alchemical project. Its theory ran from the Arabic mercury-sulphur account of metals to Newton's secret labors. In Magic, though, *Transmute* is a keyword — discard the card to search for another of equal mana value — so the word lives almost entirely in rules text: a mechanic, not a story.

sources.label: Lawrence Principe, The Secrets of Alchemy
sources.abstract: A historian of chemistry who also recreates old experiments in his own laboratory, Principe explains what alchemists actually believed and did, arguing they were serious investigators of matter and nature rather than treasure-hunting con artists, and traces how their reputation shifted over time from respected practice to pseudoscience.

hermetic.definition: Hermetic means pertaining to Hermes Trismegistus and the occult-philosophical tradition ascribed to him — and, by extension, sealed airtight. The two senses share one strange root.
hermetic.history: The name is that of *Hermes Trismegistus*, the legendary Egyptian sage credited with the *Corpus Hermeticum*. Because alchemists sealed their vessels by his art, 'hermetically sealed' came to mean airtight — your jam jar owes its seal to a magic god. The Hermetic texts, rediscovered and translated by Ficino in 1460, lit the Renaissance revival of magic — now one of the most closely studied episodes in the history of Western thought.

sources.label: Brian Copenhaver, Hermetica
sources.abstract: A fresh English translation of the Hermetica, a set of mystical Greek and Latin texts from ancient Egypt that Renaissance readers mistakenly believed were far older wisdom from a legendary sage named Hermes Trismegistus, with an introduction placing the texts in their real historical setting.

sources.label: Frances Yates, Giordano Bruno and the Hermetic Tradition
sources.abstract: A classic study arguing that the Renaissance philosopher Giordano Bruno, burned at the stake in 1600, was driven less by early modern science than by "Hermeticism" -- a set of ancient-seeming magical and mystical teachings he believed held the secret key to controlling nature.

quintessence.definition: Quintessence is the 'fifth essence' — Aristotle's incorruptible celestial element beyond earth, water, air, and fire — and, to the alchemists, the refined spirit distilled out of gross matter.
quintessence.history: From Medieval Latin *quinta essentia*, translating Aristotle's aether. John of Rupescissa, a 14th-century Franciscan, distilled alcohol and called it the quintessence, a heavenly incorruptible drawn from the earthly to fortify the body against decay. The word now means simply the purest example of a thing.

sources.label: Lyndy Abraham, A Dictionary of Alchemical Imagery
sources.abstract: A reference book that decodes the strange symbols alchemists used, like the "green lion" or the "philosopher's stone," explaining both their literal meaning as steps in a chemical process and the spiritual meaning that writers and poets later gave them, illustrated with historical woodcuts and engravings.

sources.label: Leah DeVun, Prophecy, Alchemy, and the End of Time
sources.abstract: Tells the story of a 14th-century friar, John of Rupescissa, who believed the end of the world was near and argued that alchemy could produce medicines and materials to help people survive the coming disasters.

arcane.definition: Arcane means hidden, secret, known only to initiates — the adjective of esoteric knowledge. In Magic it was also, briefly, a literal spell type.
arcane.history: Latin *arcanus*, 'shut up, secret,' from *arca*, 'a chest, a box' — the arcane is what's locked in the box (the same *arca* behind *ark*). The word carries the whole idea of magic as guarded, boxed-up knowledge. Magic's Kamigawa block made *Arcane* a spell subtype, the spirit-magic that 'Splices,' turning the adjective into a mechanic.

occult.definition: Occult means hidden — and, since the 17th century, the hidden *arts*: magic, astrology, alchemy, and their kin. An *occultist* practices them.
occult.history: Latin *occultus*, 'concealed' (past participle of *occulere*, 'to cover over'). Astronomers still say a moon *occults* a star when it passes in front and hides it — the occult is simply 'the hidden' before it was 'the sinister.' The sense 'supernatural arts' sharpens around the 1630s, and 'occultist' is a Victorian coinage, arriving with the 19th-century magical revival.

sources.label: Ronald Hutton, The Triumph of the Moon
sources.abstract: Traces how modern Wicca -- the pagan witchcraft religion that emerged in 20th-century England -- was actually assembled by blending older folklore, ceremonial magic, and Romantic ideas about nature, rather than being an unbroken survival of ancient paganism.

mystic.definition: Mystic describes the hidden spiritual truth open only to inner experience — and names one who seeks it. The word is about closed eyes and sealed lips.
mystic.history: Greek *mystikos*, from *mystēs*, 'an initiate,' from *myein*, 'to shut (the eyes or lips)' — a mystic is one who has closed to the outer world and sworn silence at the mysteries (the same root as *mystery* and *mute*). Christian, Sufi, and Kabbalist mysticisms all press toward a union beyond words. Magic's Mystics skew blue, the color of hidden knowledge.

amulet.definition: An amulet is a worn object of protection — a charm that wards off harm passively, by being carried. It contrasts with the *talisman*, which is actively charged to *do* something.
amulet.history: Latin *amuletum*, 'a charm,' of uncertain origin — perhaps from *amoliri*, 'to avert, to carry trouble away.' The amulet is the oldest wearable technology: Egyptian scarabs, Roman *bullae*, medieval saints' medals all do the same job. Magic uses 'Amulet' for protective and mana artifacts.

sources.label: Geraldine Pinch, Magic in Ancient Egypt
sources.abstract: Pinch surveys how ancient Egyptians used magic in everyday life, from protective amulets worn by pregnant women to spells recited by priests, showing that magic, religion, and medicine were not separate categories for the Egyptians but worked together as one connected system.

sigil.definition: A sigil is a drawn magical sign — a symbol, often a compressed name or figure, inscribed to focus or command a power. It is the magician's mark.
sigil.history: Latin *sigillum*, 'a little sign or seal,' diminutive of *signum*, 'sign' (the root of *signal* and *signature*). In the Solomonic tradition each spirit has its sigil, its seal of binding; RenaissanceMagicDB catalogues the *characteres* inscribed on talismans and pentacles, and John Dee's *Sigillum Dei Aemeth* is the tradition's masterpiece. Magic uses 'Sigil' widely for enchantments and tokens.

rune.definition: A rune is a letter of magical power — one of the angular characters of the Germanic writing systems, named for the secret it was thought to hold.
rune.history: Old English *rūn* means 'secret, mystery, whisper'; the runic letters were named for the hidden power that knowing them conferred, and the Norse Eddas have Odin win them in self-sacrifice. The Finnish borrowing *runo* means 'poem, song.' Magic uses 'Rune' for a cycle of Aura enchantments — magical letters fixed onto a permanent.

tome.definition: A tome is a large, weighty book — now with a connotation of ponderous learning, but originally just one volume of a longer work.
tome.history: Latin *tomus*, from Greek *tomos*, 'a slice, a section of a papyrus roll,' from *temnein*, 'to cut': a tome is literally a 'cut,' one part sliced from a larger whole. The modern sense of heft is a later inflation. In fantasy the tome is the wizard's book of power; Magic uses it for card-drawing artifacts — knowledge as a resource.

codex.definition: A codex is a book made of bound leaves — the page-turning form that replaced the scroll, and the ancestor of every modern book.
codex.history: Latin *codex* (older *caudex*), 'tree-trunk, block of wood': before paper, the codex was a stack of wax-covered wooden tablets bound at one edge. The shift from scroll to codex in late antiquity was a media revolution — random access, two-sided pages, portability. Magic uses 'Codex' for artifacts of stored knowledge.

scry.definition: To scry is to see the hidden or distant by gazing into a reflective surface — a crystal, a mirror, a bowl of water. In Magic it is the keyword for looking at the top of your library and reordering it.
scry.history: A filed-down *descry* ('to catch sight of'), from Old French *descrier*, 'to cry out'; the crystal-gazing sense dates to the 1500s and the world of John Dee's obsidian mirror. In 2004 Magic made *Scry* a keyword, giving a quiet divination word a precise new life — which is why it now lives almost entirely in rules text. The fuller story is in this term's Word History.

sources.label: Deborah Harkness, John Dee's Conversations with Angels
sources.abstract: Explores why the Elizabethan scholar John Dee spent his later years trying to talk to angels through a psychic medium, arguing these sessions grew naturally out of his earlier scientific and religious interests rather than being a sign he had lost his mind.

alchemy.definition: Alchemy is the art of transformation — above all the transmutation of base metal into gold and of the body toward perfection or immortality. Historically a genuine proto-chemistry and a spiritual discipline at once.
alchemy.history: Through Medieval Latin *alkimia* from Arabic *al-kīmiyāʾ* — the *al-* is the Arabic article, as in *algebra* and *alcohol* — over a disputed Greek root (perhaps *khēmia*, 'the Egyptian art'). The word records a transmission: Greek philosophy preserved in Arabic and handed back to a Europe that had forgotten it. See the full Word History.

sources.label: Lyndy Abraham, A Dictionary of Alchemical Imagery
sources.abstract: A reference book that decodes the strange symbols alchemists used, like the "green lion" or the "philosopher's stone," explaining both their literal meaning as steps in a chemical process and the spiritual meaning that writers and poets later gave them, illustrated with historical woodcuts and engravings.

sources.label: Lawrence Principe, The Secrets of Alchemy
sources.abstract: A historian of chemistry who also recreates old experiments in his own laboratory, Principe explains what alchemists actually believed and did, arguing they were serious investigators of matter and nature rather than treasure-hunting con artists, and traces how their reputation shifted over time from respected practice to pseudoscience.

talisman.definition: A talisman is a magical object *made* powerful by ritual — inscribed, consecrated, and charged at an elected hour to draw down a specific power. It differs from an *amulet*, which merely wards: a talisman is engineered, an amulet is worn.
talisman.history: Through Arabic *ṭilsam* from Byzantine Greek *telesma*, 'consecration' — a talisman is a *completed*, consecrated object. The astral-talisman craft is set out in the *Picatrix* and in Ficino's *De vita*: capture a planet's power in the right metal at the right hour. See the full Word History.

sources.label: Dan Attrell & David Porreca (trans.), Picatrix
sources.abstract: The first full English translation of a medieval how-to manual, originally written in Arabic and later translated into Latin, that teaches readers how to build magical charms, mix potions, and use astrology to draw on the supposed powers of the planets. It became one of the most influential sourcebooks for later European magic.

grimoire.definition: A grimoire is a book of spells — the working manual of ritual magic, listing the names, seals, and rites for summoning and commanding spirits.
grimoire.history: A French deformation of *grammaire*, 'grammar' — when learning meant Latin and looked, to the unlettered, like sorcery, the grammar-book became the spell-book. The surviving grimoires (the *Key of Solomon*, the *Lesser Key*, the *Ars Notoria*) are working manuals of names, seals, and rites. See the full Word History.

sources.label: Frank Klaassen, The Transformations of Magic
sources.abstract: This book argues that medieval magic banned by the church came in two flavors: "image magic" (making charms and talismans), which scholars could half-justify with the science of the day, and "ritual magic" (rituals for summoning spirits), which could not. Klaassen traces how interest in the first kind faded after 1500 while the second kept going and helped shape early modern magic.

sources.label: Joseph Peterson (ed.), grimoire editions
sources.abstract: Not one single book, but Joseph Peterson's long-running project (the "Twilit Grotto: Esoteric Archives" site and related small-press editions) of transcribing and translating historic "grimoires" -- old handbooks of spells, symbols, and spirit-summoning instructions -- and making them freely readable online.

conjure.definition: To conjure is to summon a spirit by binding it with an oath — and, loosely, to call anything into being as if by magic. In Magic's Alchemy format it is a keyword for creating a card from nothing.
conjure.history: Latin *coniurare*, 'to swear together' (*con-* + *iurare*) — to conjure a spirit was to bind it by a shared oath, a contract with the supernatural, the same root as *conspire* and *jury*. Magic's Alchemy format revived *Conjure* as a keyword for creating a card from nothing — the etymology turned inside out. See the full Word History.

sources.label: Richard Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages
sources.abstract: A widely used introduction to what magic actually was in medieval Europe: who practiced it, what forms it took, and how it connected to religion, science, storytelling, and law. It also explains how fear of magic fed into the witch trials of later centuries.

pentacle.definition: A pentacle is a figured, charged disc of power — of metal, parchment, or wax, inscribed with divine names and symbols and wielded in ritual. Strictly it is the whole object, not the five-pointed star alone (that is the *pentagram*).
pentacle.history: Medieval Latin *pentaculum*, on Greek *pente*, 'five.' In the Solomonic tradition each pentacle is a compressed spell — divine names and *characteres* arranged to protect the magician or compel a spirit. See the full Word History.

sources.label: Joseph Peterson (ed.), Key of Solomon
sources.abstract: Peterson's corrected and annotated edition of the most famous Renaissance-era handbook of ceremonial magic, which gives instructions for making magical tools and performing rituals, based on comparison of multiple old manuscript copies.

glamour.definition: A glamour is an illusion-spell that deceives the eye — and, faded into fashion, dazzling attractiveness. The two senses are one Scots word.
glamour.history: Astonishingly, a second mutation of *grammar* (like *grimoire*): a Scots word for 'a magic spell that deceives the eye,' popularized by Walter Scott around 1830 and softened over a century into 'dazzling attractiveness.' Call a star glamorous and you call them, at root, enchanted. See the full Word History.

phylactery.definition: A phylactery is a protective amulet or scripture-case — in Greek and Jewish usage a worn safeguard; in modern fantasy, the vessel hiding a lich's soul.
phylactery.history: Greek *phylaktērion*, 'a thing that guards,' from *phylassein*, 'to keep watch' — first an amulet, then the Jewish *tefillin*, a scripture-case worn on the body. Modern fantasy reassigned it to a lich's soul-jar, inverting the moral charge while keeping the core image of a small container of sealed power. See the full Word History.

theurgy.definition: Theurgy is 'god-working' — the high, sacramental magic by which the Neoplatonists sought union with the divine, as against the coercive low magic of *goetia*.
theurgy.history: Greek *theourgia*, 'god-working' (*theos* + *-ergon*) — the high, sacramental magic of the Neoplatonists Iamblichus and Proclus, aimed at the soul's ascent, not at commanding spirits. It became medieval magic's alibi: *we work piously toward God, not with demons*. See the full Word History.

sources.label: Claire Fanger, Invoking Angels
sources.abstract: A collection of essays about medieval and early Renaissance rituals for contacting or working with angels (scholars call this "theurgy," meaning rituals aimed at divine or heavenly beings rather than ordinary spirits). The essays show how these angel-summoning traditions mixed Christian, Jewish, and Islamic ideas together.

goetia.definition: Goetia is low magic: the summoning and commanding of demons by ritual, named from the Greek for the sorcerer's howl. It is the opposite pole to *theurgy*.
goetia.history: Greek *goēteia*, from *goēs*, 'a sorcerer who howls charms' — the low, coercive magic of binding demons, set at the bottom of the ladder opposite theurgy. Its monument is the *Ars Goetia* of the *Lesser Key of Solomon*, with its seventy-two named demons and their seals. See the full Word History.

sources.label: Joseph Peterson (ed.), The Lesser Key of Solomon
sources.abstract: A modern scholarly edition of a 17th-century handbook of magic (called a "grimoire") that lists methods and diagrams for summoning and commanding spirits, put together and corrected by Peterson from several old manuscript copies held in the British Library.

exorcism.definition: Exorcism is the ritual casting-out of a spirit — driving out a demon not by force but by binding it with a stronger, holier oath.
exorcism.history: Greek *exorkismos*, 'a binding-out by oath' (*ex-* + *horkos*, 'oath') — you cast out a spirit not by force but by a superior oath, invoking a higher authority. In practice the exorcist and the conjurer often read from the same page. See the full Word History.

sources.label: Frank Klaassen, The Transformations of Magic
sources.abstract: This book argues that medieval magic banned by the church came in two flavors: "image magic" (making charms and talismans), which scholars could half-justify with the science of the day, and "ritual magic" (rituals for summoning spirits), which could not. Klaassen traces how interest in the first kind faded after 1500 while the second kept going and helped shape early modern magic.

abjure.definition: To abjure is to renounce something under oath — a faith, an error, a spirit's claim — formally forswearing what one once held or what threatens one. Where to conjure binds a power by swearing it in, to abjure repudiates by swearing it out; it is the oath run in reverse, disavowal made binding.
abjure.history: The root is legal Latin *abiurare*, 'to deny on oath' (*ab-*, 'away from', + *iurare*, 'to swear') — the exact inverse of conjuring, which binds by an oath sworn together. The turn was ecclesiastical: to abjure became the courtroom act of recanting heresy or witchcraft. In Tudor Wells, one Katherine Love stood before the vicar-general to 'curse, damn, and abjure' her charms; Menocchio's inquisitors closed his trial with a lengthy abjuration. So the word carried a double charge — the exorcist's *I abjure you* commanding a demon out, and the penitent's oath surrendering forbidden knowledge. Magic keeps the warding sense: abjuration is the school of protective, dispelling spells.

sources.label: Wright & Klaassen, Everyday Magicians (abjuration formula, Wells)
sources.abstract: Drawing on Tudor-era court records and surviving spellbooks, this book argues that most people practicing magic in 16th-century England were ordinary neighbors solving everyday problems -- money troubles, love, theft, sickness -- and were treated by the law as minor offenders, not the dangerous witches later legend made them out to be.

sources.label: Ginzburg, The Cheese and the Worms (Menocchio's abjuration)
sources.abstract: Ginzburg reconstructs the strange private worldview of Menocchio, a 16th-century Italian miller put on trial by the Inquisition for claiming the universe formed the way cheese curdles, with worms (which he equated with angels) appearing inside it, using the case to explore how ordinary people quietly reworked religious ideas in their own terms.

banish.definition: To banish is to drive a being out and forbid its return — a spirit to the infernal regions, a person from the realm, a demon from a body. It differs from a mere summoning-in-reverse: banishment carries the force of proclamation and prohibition, an expulsion declared as much as enacted, closing the door behind what it casts out.
banish.history: The word comes through Old French *banir* from Frankish *bannjan*, 'to proclaim, to summon by ban' — the same *bann* behind 'ban' and the marriage 'banns,' so exile began as a public shout of expulsion. The turn is that this civic proclamation became a ritual one: what a court did to an outlaw, the exorcist and magician did to spirits. Crowley's Goetia describes the old gods 'banished to the infernal regions' by a new order; monks banished demons by invoking Mary or making the cross. From decree to dispelling, the sense holds. Magic uses *Banish* as a keyword — removing a permanent from the game, sometimes for good.

sources.label: Crowley et al., Illustrated Goetia (gods banished to the infernal regions)
sources.abstract: A modern, illustrated update of occultist Aleister Crowley's 1904 translation of a famous old demon-summoning manual known as the Goetia. Later editors add plain-language commentary and pictures of the 72 spirits so contemporary readers can understand a ritual system that originally assumed a lot of specialized background knowledge.

sources.label: Hanegraaff et al., Hermes Explains (banishing demons by invocation)
sources.abstract: Thirty scholars each answer a basic question about "Western esotericism" -- the umbrella term for alternative religious and philosophical traditions like alchemy, astrology, and magic -- aiming to explain the field in plain terms to readers who have never studied it before.

bewitch.definition: To bewitch is to work witchcraft upon someone — to lay a spell that binds, sickens, or entrances, often against the victim's will. Unlike a charm that merely delights, bewitching is charged with agency and harm: a cow struck barren, a king made pliant, a lover ensnared. It names the effect from the sufferer's side, the state of being under another's craft.
bewitch.history: The word is Old English *wiccian*, 'to work magic,' with the intensifying prefix *be-*: to witch someone, harder. Its medieval life ran on *maleficium*, the language of harm — Kieckhefer notes the commonest charge in the courts was that an old woman had 'bewitched his cows or his children.' The turn is a softening: the diplomat Chapuys claimed Anne Boleyn had so 'enchanted and bewitched' Henry VIII he would deny her nothing, and by then the word could mean fascination as easily as injury. So a term of injury became one of allure — and, in Magic, *Bewitch* is a keyword that binds an Aura's fate to an enchanted creature.

sources.label: Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages (bewitched cows and children)
sources.abstract: A widely used introduction to what magic actually was in medieval Europe: who practiced it, what forms it took, and how it connected to religion, science, storytelling, and law. It also explains how fear of magic fed into the witch trials of later centuries.

bless.definition: To bless is to draw favor down upon a person, object, or act — to sanctify by word and gesture, invoking a higher power's protection or grace. It differs from consecrating: to consecrate sets a thing wholly apart as sacred, while to bless confers benefit and goodwill, a benediction that can fall on a candle, a field, a departing soul, or a meal.
bless.history: Astonishingly, the root is bloody: Old English *blēdsian*, 'to consecrate with blood,' from *blōd* — a pagan rite of sprinkling an altar with sacrifice. The turn came with Christianity, which redirected the word to translate Latin *benedicere*, 'to speak well of,' so a splash of gore became a spoken benediction. The medieval Church built, as Keith Thomas notes, 'a comprehensive range of formulae' to draw God's blessing onto salt, water, candles, and stones — Klaassen records the blessing of a stone to restore the virtue it had before Adam and Eve's Fall from Eden. The blood drained out of the word entirely; only the favor remained. Magic keeps the martial-devotional sense — blessings that shield and strengthen.

sources.label: Thomas, Religion and the Decline of Magic (formulae to draw down blessing)
sources.abstract: A landmark history of why belief in witchcraft, astrology, and protective charms was so common in 16th- and 17th-century England, and how the rise of Protestant religion and early science gradually pushed those beliefs to the margins of everyday life.

sources.label: Klaassen, The Transformations of Magic (blessing of a stone)
sources.abstract: This book argues that medieval magic banned by the church came in two flavors: "image magic" (making charms and talismans), which scholars could half-justify with the science of the day, and "ritual magic" (rituals for summoning spirits), which could not. Klaassen traces how interest in the first kind faded after 1500 while the second kept going and helped shape early modern magic.

chant.definition: To chant is to utter words in measured, repeated song — a ritual formula voiced aloud so that its rhythm and sound become part of its power. It differs from ordinary prayer or speech in its cadence: chanting foregrounds the acoustic body of the words, the intoned repetition through which a spell, a psalm, or a name is made to work.
chant.history: The root is Latin *cantare*, 'to sing' (a frequentative of *canere*) — the same song hiding inside *enchant*, *incant*, and *incantation*, half the vocabulary of magic traced back to sung Latin. The turn is theoretical: Renaissance magi asked *why* chanting worked, debating whether *incantatio* acted through the natural acoustic properties of sound as a vehicle of *spiritus*, through divine names, or through demons — the very question that divided natural magic from the illicit. The Greek papyri already summon gods 'with this chant,' and monastics chanted psalms to drive out spirits. Sung words, in short, were never merely decorative; Magic's *Chant* effects keep that ritual, repeating pulse.

sources.label: RenaissanceMagicDB, s.v. incantatio (chanted words producing magical effects)
Betz, The Greek Magical Papyri in Translation (call the god with this chant)
sources.abstract: A translated collection of real spells, charms, and magical recipes written on papyrus in Greco-Roman Egypt roughly 1,600 to 2,200 years ago, giving readers direct access to what ordinary magical practice actually looked like rather than what critics said about it.

charm.definition: A charm is a spoken or written formula — a small verbal spell for healing, protection, or binding; to charm is to work such words upon someone, captivating or compelling them. It differs from enchantment in scale and register: a charm is the short, portable, everyday formula, often a scrap of prayer or verse, where enchantment implies a fuller, more theatrical working.
charm.history: A charm is literally a song: Latin *carmen*, 'song, verse, incantation' — the same *carmen* as *Carmina Burana*. The turn is downward and outward: what began as sung verse became, in medieval practice, the humblest and commonest magic of all. As *The Magic of Rogues* observes, the most widespread form of magic in the middle ages was the use of charms, short formulae usually lifted from Scripture or liturgy for common ailments — mobile textual fragments that scholars can barely tell from prayer, medicine, or superstition. From there it is a short slide to the modern sense: a 'charming' person is, etymologically, still singing one over you. Magic scatters the word across dozens of cards.

sources.label: MedievalMagicDB, s.v. Charms (spoken/written formulae for healing, protection, coercion)
The Magic of Rogues (charms the most widespread medieval magic)
sources.abstract: Using real 16th-century English court records, this book tells the true stories of two magic scandals: a group hunting for buried treasure with a spell that went wrong, and a magician swept into a treason case with his employer. It shows that everyday folk magic was usually punished lightly, not treated like the witch trials people imagine.

commune.definition: To commune is to enter into shared, intimate contact with spirits, gods, or nature — to hold something in common with them, exchanging presence rather than commands. It differs from conjuring: to conjure is to summon and bind a spirit to one's will, whereas to commune is to join with it, a reciprocal opening toward the divine or the departed rather than a compulsion of it.
commune.history: The root is Latin *communicare*, 'to share,' from *communis*, 'common' — the same root as *communion* and *community*, so communing with spirits is, at bottom, holding something in common with them. The turn is contemplative: in the Neoplatonic and theurgic tradition that shaped Renaissance magic, *theurgia* aimed precisely at *koinōnia*, Iamblichus's 'communion of the gods with men,' the soul's ascent to unmediated contact rather than the coercion of *goetia*. A fourteenth-century monk on record claimed to have communed with spirits for thirty years; Dee sought communion with angels. Sharing, not summoning, is the key. Magic's *Commune with Nature* and kin keep that gentler, joining sense.

sources.label: RenaissanceMagicDB, s.v. theurgia (ritual ascent to communion with the divine)
Meyer & Mirecki, Magic and Ritual in the Ancient World (theurgic communion of gods with men)
sources.abstract: This edited collection of scholarly essays asks what counted as "magic" (as opposed to accepted religion) across the ancient Near East, Greece, Rome, Judaism, and early Christianity and Islam, using real spell texts, amulets, and rituals to explore where societies drew that line and why.

sources.label: Fanger, Invoking Angels (communed with spirits for thirty years)
sources.abstract: A collection of essays about medieval and early Renaissance rituals for contacting or working with angels (scholars call this "theurgy," meaning rituals aimed at divine or heavenly beings rather than ordinary spirits). The essays show how these angel-summoning traditions mixed Christian, Jewish, and Islamic ideas together.

consecrate.definition: To *consecrate* is to make a person, place, or object sacred through rite — dedicating it to a god or divine power and setting it apart from ordinary use. In magic it charges an instrument for operation: where to *bless* asks favor upon a thing, to *consecrate* transfers it into the sacred order entirely. Grimoires consecrate the sword, circle, and lamen before any spirit is called.
consecrate.history: Latin *consecrare* joined *com-* to *sacer*, 'sacred,' meaning 'to make wholly sacred' — to render a thing over to the gods. The turn is that *sacer* was double-edged, naming both the holy and the accursed (whence *execrate*); the Romans knew the sacred was dangerous to touch. Ritual magic inherited the operative sense: a circle must be constructed on cleared, consecrated ground, and grimoires like the *Lesser Key of Solomon* consecrate figures, swords, and equipment before use. Magic: The Gathering keeps the plain sanctifying sense, consecrating ground and relics.

sources.label: RenaissanceMagicDB, s.v. lamina (consecration of inscribed plates)
Klaassen, The Transformations of Magic (consecrated circle)
sources.abstract: This book argues that medieval magic banned by the church came in two flavors: "image magic" (making charms and talismans), which scholars could half-justify with the science of the day, and "ritual magic" (rituals for summoning spirits), which could not. Klaassen traces how interest in the first kind faded after 1500 while the second kept going and helped shape early modern magic.

evoke.definition: To *evoke* is to call a spirit or power *out* — to summon it forth into appearance before the operator, typically into a circle, triangle, or vessel outside the self. It is the mirror of *invoke*, which draws a power *in*: the ceremonial magician evokes a demon to visible station, but invokes a god into their own person. Evocation makes the spirit manifest and external.
evoke.history: Latin *evocare* joined *e-*, 'out,' to *vocare*, 'to call' — literally 'to call out.' The sharpest turn is Roman: the ritual of *evocatio* called an enemy city's tutelary gods *out* of its walls to defect before a siege, state-sponsored divine poaching. Ceremonial magic preserved the directional precision — Solomonic handbooks describe aerial spirits evoked and constrained into circles — so that to evoke means to bring a spirit out and make it appear. English later softened it toward 'call forth a feeling or image,' but magic keeps the older, literal summons.

sources.label: Peterson, The Lesser Key of Solomon (spirits evoked and constrained)
sources.abstract: A modern scholarly edition of a 17th-century handbook of magic (called a "grimoire") that lists methods and diagrams for summoning and commanding spirits, put together and corrected by Peterson from several old manuscript copies held in the British Library.

haunt.definition: To *haunt* is for a spirit or the dead to trouble a place or person by lingering there — returning, unbidden, to a house, a road, or a grave. It differs from *possess*, which enters and inhabits a living body: a haunting clings to a location and disturbs from outside, where possession seizes a self from within. The ghost haunts because it will not leave.
haunt.history: Old French *hanter* meant simply 'to frequent, resort to' — to keep going somewhere — with no ghost in it; a hunter's *haunts* were just the places game gathered. The turn is late and English: because the returning dead were imagined as spirits who *kept frequenting* the places they had known, 'haunt' took on its spectral sense. Renaissance sources still show both — Keith Thomas records the belief in ghosts revisiting their haunts from purgatory, and Zambelli notes husbands' ghosts who came back to haunt their widows. Magic: The Gathering makes it a keyword for spirits that cling on.

sources.label: Thomas, Religion and the Decline of Magic (ghosts revisiting their haunts)
sources.abstract: A landmark history of why belief in witchcraft, astrology, and protective charms was so common in 16th- and 17th-century England, and how the rise of Protestant religion and early science gradually pushed those beliefs to the margins of everyday life.

sources.label: Paola Zambelli, White Magic, Black Magic in the European Renaissance (ghosts returning to haunt)
sources.abstract: Examines Renaissance thinkers' theories about magic, focusing especially on how learned 'high' magic practiced by scholars overlapped and interacted with everyday popular magic and witchcraft accusations among ordinary people.

incant.definition: To *incant* is to produce a magical effect by chanting or reciting words of power — set formulas, divine names, or *voces magicae* — sung or spoken over a person, image, or object. It names the verbal engine of a rite rather than its whole procedure: where a *charm* is the binding song itself, to incant is the act of pronouncing it. The incantation does the work that gesture and material alone cannot.
incant.history: Latin *incantare* meant 'to sing a spell upon' (*in-* + *cantare*, 'to sing') — the same word that, worn smooth through French, became 'enchant.' The turn is that English borrowed it *twice*: once early and softened into *enchant*, once late and bookish as *incant*, so incantation and enchantment are one Latin word wearing two faces. The learned form stayed technical — *incantatio* is among the commonest terms in the Renaissance magical corpus, the ritual use of chanted words to invoke spirits or channel celestial influence.

sources.label: Attrell & Porreca, Picatrix (Aristotle on magical incantations)
sources.abstract: The first full English translation of a medieval how-to manual, originally written in Arabic and later translated into Latin, that teaches readers how to build magical charms, mix potions, and use astrology to draw on the supposed powers of the planets. It became one of the most influential sourcebooks for later European magic.

invoke.definition: To *invoke* is to call upon a higher power — a god, angel, spirit, or saint — by name, asking it to attend, aid, or descend into the operator. It is the exact counterpart of *evoke*: invocation calls a power *in* (into the self, a statue, or a name), evocation calls a spirit *out* into external appearance. The magus invokes the divine but evokes the demon into the circle.
invoke.history: Latin *invocare* joined *in-* to *vocare*, 'to call,' meaning 'to call upon.' The pivotal refinement is ceremonial: practitioners fixed *invocare* against *evocare* as opposite directions of address — to call a power into oneself versus to call a spirit out — a distinction the theurgic tradition, addressing angels and gods, kept precise. The Greek Magical Papyri open rites with 'invoke in this way,' and *invocatio* stands among the most frequent operations in the Renaissance corpus. Magic: The Gathering uses it loosely for calling a power to one's side.

sources.label: Fanger, Invoking Angels (spirits invoked and addressed)
sources.abstract: A collection of essays about medieval and early Renaissance rituals for contacting or working with angels (scholars call this "theurgy," meaning rituals aimed at divine or heavenly beings rather than ordinary spirits). The essays show how these angel-summoning traditions mixed Christian, Jewish, and Islamic ideas together.

possess.definition: To *possess*, in the magical sense, is for a spirit or demon to seize and inhabit a living body, overriding its owner's will and acting through it. It differs from *haunt*, which lingers about a place from outside: possession takes up residence *within* a person. The word carries its property-law twin — the possessing spirit occupies the body as a squatter holds ground it does not own.
possess.history: Latin *possidēre*, 'to hold or occupy' (from *potis*, 'able,' + *sedēre*, 'to sit'), meant to have and hold — the root of 'property' and 'possession.' The turn is Christian: demonic *possession* recast that legal 'holding' as a spirit literally *sitting in* a body, an occupying tenant to be expelled. The medieval and Renaissance record treats it as a clinical category: one saint's life, on one modern scholarly account, began as a fairly ordinary case of demonic possession, and reformers staged spectacular exorcisms of those considered possessed by demons. Magic: The Gathering keeps the seizing, body-taking sense.

sources.label: David J. Collins, S.J. (ed.), The Sacred and the Sinister: Studies in Medieval Religion and Magic (case of demonic possession)
sources.abstract: A collection of essays exploring how medieval people tried to draw a line between approved religious practice and forbidden magic — and how blurry and contested that line actually was in everyday life.

sources.label: Paola Zambelli, White Magic, Black Magic in the European Renaissance (exorcism of those possessed by demons)
sources.abstract: Examines Renaissance thinkers' theories about magic, focusing especially on how learned 'high' magic practiced by scholars overlapped and interacted with everyday popular magic and witchcraft accusations among ordinary people.

amalgam.definition: An *amalgam* is an alloy formed by dissolving another metal — gold, silver, tin — into liquid mercury, yielding a soft, workable mass. Where ordinary alloys are fused in the fierce heat of the crucible, an amalgam forms almost cold, mercury swallowing its partner. The alchemists prized the gold amalgam as a step toward the philosophers' stone; the word later widened to any fusion of disparate things.
amalgam.history: Medieval Latin *amalgama* — probably from Arabic *al-malgham*, 'emollient, soft mass' — names the pliant paste mercury makes when it takes up another metal, one more Arabic *al-* word smuggled into the science of metals. Alchemical writers kept it literal: the *Mutus Liber* speaks of the philosophic amalgam of mercury, gold and the silver of art. The turn is metaphorical and late: by the seventeenth century any blending of unlike elements — of doctrines, peoples, notions — could be called an amalgam, and scholars now write of Hermetism as an Hellenistic amalgam of Platonism, Stoicism, and Judaism.

sources.label: McLean, A Commentary on the Mutus Liber
sources.abstract: A plate-by-plate guide to the Mutus Liber ("Wordless Book"), a strange 17th-century alchemy book made almost entirely of pictures with no text, walking through what each mysterious engraving might mean.

sources.label: Paola Zambelli, White Magic, Black Magic in the European Renaissance
sources.abstract: Examines Renaissance thinkers' theories about magic, focusing especially on how learned 'high' magic practiced by scholars overlapped and interacted with everyday popular magic and witchcraft accusations among ordinary people.

crucible.definition: A *crucible* is a small, thick-walled vessel of clay or fired ceramic built to hold metals and minerals over a fierce open fire until they fuse. Unlike a *retort* or *alembic*, which carry off vapors, the crucible keeps its charge in place to melt, mix, and purify. It is the shared workbench of assayers, smelters, and alchemists.
crucible.history: Medieval Latin *crucibulum* meant a melting-pot — and also a night-lamp — and medieval scribes, hearing *crux*, 'cross,' half-linked the little pot to an ordeal of fire. The turn is the metaphor that outlived the apparatus. Renaissance readers of Dee and Ashmole cast the alchemist himself as a kind of crucible wherein the soul is purified along with the object of his external operations; Pico threw all doctrines into the crucible of his thought. From that image comes our sense of a crucible as any severe test that refines what passes through it — the vessel's fire lent to trials of the spirit.

sources.label: Brann, on the Fasciculus chemicus (Arthur Dee / Ashmole / Abraham)
sources.abstract: This is a scholarly review by Noel Brann of a modern edition of the Fasciculus Chemicus, a 17th-century anthology of alchemical writings put together by Arthur Dee (son of the famous Elizabethan scholar John Dee) and translated into English by Elias Ashmole, discussing what the collection reveals about English alchemical thought at the time.

sources.label: Attrell & Porreca, Picatrix: A Medieval Treatise on Astral Magic
sources.abstract: The first full English translation of a medieval how-to manual, originally written in Arabic and later translated into Latin, that teaches readers how to build magical charms, mix potions, and use astrology to draw on the supposed powers of the planets. It became one of the most influential sourcebooks for later European magic.

distill.definition: To *distill* is to heat a liquid until it vaporizes, then cool the vapor so it condenses and drips back pure into a receiver, leaving the dross behind. It is separation by rising and falling — distinct from *sublimation*, in which a solid passes straight to vapor without melting. In the alchemist's alembic and retort, distillation drew off spirits, essential oils, and the burning *aqua ardens* of wine.
distill.history: Latin *destillare*, 'to drip down' (*de-* plus *stillare*, 'to drip,' from *stilla*, 'a drop'), names the process by its humblest sign: the steady fall of purified drops from the still's beak. Greek alchemy built the apparatus early — the *ambix*, through Arabic *al-anbiq*, gives us 'alembic.' The turn is figurative and durable: to distill became to reduce anything to its essence. Kieckhefer calls a medieval leechbook a reasonably sober distillation of classical medicine, and Renaissance writers say Agrippa skilfully distilled his magic from Ficino and Pico. Magic: The Gathering keeps both the vessel's steam and the metaphor's purity.

sources.label: Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages
sources.abstract: A widely used introduction to what magic actually was in medieval Europe: who practiced it, what forms it took, and how it connected to religion, science, storytelling, and law. It also explains how fear of magic fed into the witch trials of later centuries.

sources.label: Greco-Egyptian alchemy (on the ambix / alembic)
sources.abstract: Traces how alchemy first took shape in ancient Alexandria, Egypt, where Greek philosophical ideas blended with hands-on Egyptian craft knowledge like metalworking and dyeing, forming the roots of what would eventually become chemistry.

potion.definition: A *potion* is a prepared draught meant to be drunk for magical or medicinal effect — a love-philter, a sleeping-drink, a cure, a poison. Where a *tincture* is an extracted essence and a talisman is worn, a potion is swallowed, its power entering the body directly. The category slides easily from healing to harm: Circe's cup and the physician's cordial are, in form, the same object.
potion.history: Latin *potio*, 'a drink' (from *potare*, 'to drink'), is the plain root — and its twin. The very same word, traveling a different French road, becomes 'poison,' so that potion and poison are perfect etymological doubles, and the sources honor the pairing: the Greek magical papyri promise one who is unaffected by potions and poison. The medical and the maleficent never fully part. Renaissance recipe books record purgative 'potions,' while witchcraft trials turn on the 'love potion,' the *pharmaka* that heal, madden, or kill by the same draught.

sources.label: Betz, The Greek Magical Papyri in Translation
sources.abstract: A translated collection of real spells, charms, and magical recipes written on papyrus in Greco-Roman Egypt roughly 1,600 to 2,200 years ago, giving readers direct access to what ordinary magical practice actually looked like rather than what critics said about it.

sources.label: Parry, The Arch Conjuror of England: John Dee
sources.abstract: A biography arguing that John Dee was not just an eccentric magician on the sidelines of history, but a real political operator whose magic and angel-conversations were tangled up with Elizabethan court intrigue and Europe's religious conflicts.

putrefy.definition: To *putrefy* is to let matter rot, breaking down its form so a new one can arise — the deliberate, controlled decay of the alchemical *nigredo*, the blackening. In the sequence of operations it follows dissolution and precedes coagulation: the sealed vessel becomes a 'grave' where the Stone's matter dies and blackens. Where calcination burns and distillation refines, putrefaction destroys — but destroys on purpose, as the first necessary step.
putrefy.history: Latin *putrefacere*, 'to make rotten' (*puter*, 'rotten,' plus *facere*, 'to make'), names the alchemists' *nigredo* — the black rotting stage that had to come before any perfection. The turn is doctrinal: rot became a rung on the ladder up. Alchemical vessels during the dissolution, death, and putrefaction of the Stone's matter were called the coffin, grave, and prison, and, as one commentary on the *Mutus Liber* insists, the putrefaction gives rise to a succession of colors, the first of which, the black, is the key to the others. Jung's readers still speak of the *nigredo* as a forceful putrefaction — the suppression of the ego before renewal.

sources.label: Abraham, A Dictionary of Alchemical Imagery
sources.abstract: A reference book that decodes the strange symbols alchemists used, like the "green lion" or the "philosopher's stone," explaining both their literal meaning as steps in a chemical process and the spiritual meaning that writers and poets later gave them, illustrated with historical woodcuts and engravings.

sources.label: McLean, A Commentary on the Mutus Liber
sources.abstract: A plate-by-plate guide to the Mutus Liber ("Wordless Book"), a strange 17th-century alchemy book made almost entirely of pictures with no text, walking through what each mysterious engraving might mean.

retort.definition: A *retort* is a glass or ceramic distilling vessel with a rounded body and a long neck bent sharply back and down, so that vapor driven off the heated body travels along the bend and condenses into a receiver. The curved neck is its signature: where an *alembic* wears a separate cap and delivery-tube, the retort is a single bent piece. Alchemists charged it with sulphur, vitriol, and ores to drive off acids.
retort.history: Latin *retortus*, 'twisted back' (*re-* plus *torquere*, 'to twist'), describes exactly the vessel's doubled-back neck. The turn is a happy pun the language kept: the *retort* that means a sharp reply is the same word — an answer twisted back at the speaker. Excavated alchemical laboratories yield retorts, vessels, trays, alembics, and phials, and Ben Jonson's *Alchemist* lists 'Retorts, Receivers, Pellicanes, Bolt-heads' all struck to shivers when the furnace bursts. Magic: The Gathering inherits the flask, curved neck and all, as an emblem of the laboratory.

sources.label: Page & Rider, The Routledge History of Medieval Magic
sources.abstract: A large reference collection that rounds up current scholarship on magic in medieval Europe (roughly 1100 to 1500), covering the texts people used, how the church and universities reacted, and how magic touched gender roles, art, medicine, and the early witch trials.

sources.label: Abraham, A Dictionary of Alchemical Imagery (on Jonson)
sources.abstract: A reference book that decodes the strange symbols alchemists used, like the "green lion" or the "philosopher's stone," explaining both their literal meaning as steps in a chemical process and the spiritual meaning that writers and poets later gave them, illustrated with historical woodcuts and engravings.

sublimate.definition: To *sublimate* is to heat a solid until it turns straight to vapor, skipping the liquid state, then let it recondense as a purified solid higher in the vessel — a rising and refining in one motion. It differs from *distillation*, which passes through a liquid and drips down; sublimation lifts a dry substance up. Alchemists sublimated mercury, sulphur, and arsenic to separate their 'spirits' from their gross bodies.
sublimate.history: Latin *sublimare*, 'to raise up' (from *sublimis*, 'lofty'), fit an operation that literally elevates matter — Zosimos used fire in distillation, sublimation, and volatilization to separate the spirits from the bodies. The turn is a borrowing outward. Renaissance Neoplatonists already spoke of a sublimation of the soul and of desire spiritualized; Freud then took the word for psychic life, naming *sublimation* the redirecting of instinct from a base to a noble object. So the alembic's rising vapor became the analyst's model for desire raised toward higher ends — the operation carried, unbroken, from furnace to couch.

sources.label: Hanegraaff, Hermetic Spirituality and the Historical Imagination (on Zosimos)
sources.abstract: Looks at the ancient Egyptian writings attributed to the legendary teacher Hermes Trismegistus and argues they weren't just abstract philosophy — they describe real spiritual exercises meant to shift a person's state of mind so they could see past ordinary illusions to a deeper truth about reality.

tincture.definition: A *tincture* is a colored essence drawn out of a substance — and, in alchemy, more: a preparation that dyes a base metal while imparting its virtue, the coloring agent of transmutation itself. Unlike a mere *potion* to be drunk, a tincture acts by tinting: to redden a metal to gold is to give it the highest tincture. Color and power ride together, the hue carrying the essence.
tincture.history: Latin *tinctura*, 'a dyeing' (from *tingere*, 'to dye, to moisten'), begins in the dye-vat. Alchemy raised it: the tincture of the Sages was the reddening power that turned metals to gold, the Stone itself understood as color made potent. Paracelsus wrote a whole *Book Concerning the Tincture of the Philosophers*, and Donne could call the risen Christ 'all tincture,' able to transmute leaden and iron wills to good. Heraldry keeps the plainer sense — a shield's tinctures are its colors — so the same word names both the dyer's bath and the philosophers' gold-making essence.

sources.label: Abraham, A Dictionary of Alchemical Imagery (on Donne)
sources.abstract: A reference book that decodes the strange symbols alchemists used, like the "green lion" or the "philosopher's stone," explaining both their literal meaning as steps in a chemical process and the spiritual meaning that writers and poets later gave them, illustrated with historical woodcuts and engravings.

sources.label: Mebane, Renaissance Magic and the Return of the Golden Age (on Paracelsus)
sources.abstract: Mebane argues that Renaissance ideas about magic - the belief that a learned person could unlock hidden powers in nature - shaped major English plays like Doctor Faustus, The Alchemist, and The Tempest, and even fed contemporary hopes for moral and scientific progress.

vitriol.definition: *Vitriol* is the alchemists' name for the sulphate salts — most familiarly blue vitriol (copper sulphate) and green vitriol (iron sulphate) — glassy, brightly colored crystals, and the corrosive acids distilled from them. It was a workhorse reagent: ground for pigments, roasted and distilled to yield mineral acids. Where an amalgam is soft and metallic, vitriol is a crystalline salt, prized for its color and its bite.
vitriol.history: Medieval Latin *vitriolum*, from Latin *vitrum*, 'glass,' named the salt for its clear, glassy crystals. The turn runs two ways. In the laboratory vitriol became a master material — the *Picatrix* grinds blue vitriol and talc for a golden pigment, and alchemists coded it as the 'Green Lion,' a key material in the Work. In speech the same corrosive salt gave us *vitriolic*: sharp, acid words, as when a humanist's vitriolic *elogium* or a scholar's vitriolic pen burned an opponent. The glassy salt still bites in the metaphor.

sources.label: Attrell & Porreca, Picatrix: A Medieval Treatise on Astral Magic
sources.abstract: The first full English translation of a medieval how-to manual, originally written in Arabic and later translated into Latin, that teaches readers how to build magical charms, mix potions, and use astrology to draw on the supposed powers of the planets. It became one of the most influential sourcebooks for later European magic.

sources.label: Paola Zambelli, White Magic, Black Magic in the European Renaissance
sources.abstract: Examines Renaissance thinkers' theories about magic, focusing especially on how learned 'high' magic practiced by scholars overlapped and interacted with everyday popular magic and witchcraft accusations among ordinary people.

bestiary.definition: A *bestiary* is a book of beasts: a catalogue of animals, real and fabulous, in which each creature is described chiefly for the moral or spiritual lesson it carries. Where an *herbal* gathers plants and their virtues, the bestiary gathers animals as allegory, treating zoology as a branch of theology rather than natural history.
bestiary.history: From Latin *bestia*, 'beast', plus *-arium*, 'a place or collection for': a gathering of beasts. The turn is that the medieval bestiary made every animal a sermon — the pelican reviving its young stood for Christ, the ant's industry for virtue. Ron Baxter has argued that houses such as St. Augustine's promoted bestiaries as *sermon exempla*, their parallels between animal customs and human vice running deep into preaching. The genre lends its name to many a Magic card, where a book of beasts is once again a bestiary of the imagination.

sources.label: Frank Klaassen, The Transformations of Magic
sources.abstract: This book argues that medieval magic banned by the church came in two flavors: "image magic" (making charms and talismans), which scholars could half-justify with the science of the day, and "ritual magic" (rituals for summoning spirits), which could not. Klaassen traces how interest in the first kind faded after 1500 while the second kept going and helped shape early modern magic.

sources.label: Sophie Page, Magic in the Cloister
sources.abstract: A study of English monks who, despite their religious vows, collected and studied magic texts as part of their intellectual curiosity. It shows that for these monks, magic and monastic religious life weren't necessarily opposites — they could fit together.

book-of-hours.definition: A book of hours is a lay devotional day-book: a collection of prayers, psalms, and offices arranged to be recited at the canonical hours of the day. Unlike a monk's breviary, it was made for private devotion by ordinary people, and its calendar structure made it the household prayer book of the later Middle Ages.
book-of-hours.history: The name renders Latin *horae*, 'hours' (ultimately Greek *hōra*, 'season, time of day'), the fixed intervals at which the Church appointed prayer. The turn is one of sheer numbers: the book of hours was the runaway bestseller of the Middle Ages, produced in vast quantity and, at its finest, the chief canvas for the illuminator's art. It brought the daily rhythm of monastic prayer into the layperson's hands, and made the private book itself an instrument of piety.

sources.label: Etymonline, s.v. hour (Latin horae ← Greek hōra)

folio.definition: A *folio* is a leaf of a book — a single sheet counted front and back — and, by extension, a large book made of full sheets folded once. As a format it names the grandest, most expensive class of volume; as a unit it is how manuscripts are measured and cited, leaf by leaf (fol. 18r, 28v) rather than by page.
folio.history: From Latin *folium*, 'a leaf', the same root behind *foliage* and *portfolio*, a leaf-carrier. Before pagination, scribes numbered a book's leaves rather than its pages, so scholars still cite a manuscript by folio and side — recto and verso. The turn came with print, where 'folio' named the largest and most imposing format: Gesner's *Historia animalium* ran to more than 4,500 folio pages, and Shakespeare's collected plays survive as the First Folio.

sources.label: Håkansson, Seeing the Word: John Dee and Renaissance Occultism
sources.abstract: A doctoral thesis arguing that John Dee's strange mix of interests -- grammar, the Kabbalah, astronomy, alchemy, and math -- were not scattered hobbies but one connected project: recovering a lost perfect language Dee believed could unlock both nature's secrets and God's truth.

sources.label: Claire Fanger, Invoking Angels
sources.abstract: A collection of essays about medieval and early Renaissance rituals for contacting or working with angels (scholars call this "theurgy," meaning rituals aimed at divine or heavenly beings rather than ordinary spirits). The essays show how these angel-summoning traditions mixed Christian, Jewish, and Islamic ideas together.

herbal.definition: An *herbal* is a book of plants and their virtues: a catalogue describing each herb, how to gather and prepare it, and what it cures. It sits between the *bestiary* (beasts) and the *lapidary* (stones) as one of the three great books of natural lore, and in it botany, medicine, and magic were bound in a single volume.
herbal.history: From Latin *herba*, 'grass, herb', plus *-al*, 'a book concerning'. The turn is that the herbal never separated healing from lore: as Kieckhefer notes, herbals — books about medicinal herbs — grew a lush growth of lore about the wondrous powers of plants, prescribing ritual methods for picking as readily as recipes for curing. Stephen Skinner observes that herbals, bestiaries, and lapidaries often split off from the grimoire itself, the correspondences of plants, animals, and stones carved out of a single body of magical knowledge.

sources.label: Richard Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages
sources.abstract: A widely used introduction to what magic actually was in medieval Europe: who practiced it, what forms it took, and how it connected to religion, science, storytelling, and law. It also explains how fear of magic fed into the witch trials of later centuries.

sources.label: Stephen Skinner, Magical Techniques and Implements
sources.abstract: Skinner's doctoral thesis traces specific magic tools and step-by-step methods (like ritual instruments and spirit-summoning procedures) as they passed from ancient Greek-Egyptian magic texts, through Byzantine Greek manuscripts, into later European handbooks of magic — showing one continuous tradition rather than separate inventions.

manuscript.definition: A *manuscript* is a book or document written by hand rather than printed. The word defines a thing entirely by its mode of making, and in the study of magic it names the primary evidence: the copied, recopied, and recombined ritual books that carried the tradition. Abbreviated *MS* (plural *MSS*).
manuscript.history: From Latin *manu scriptus*, 'written by hand' (*manus*, 'hand', plus *scribere*, 'to write'). The turn is that the term only becomes meaningful in pointed contrast to print — before movable type every book was hand-copied, so no one needed the word. Scholars of learned magic insist the field is above all a manuscript ecology: texts such as the *Ars Notoria* survived not as fixed books but as flexible dossiers, copied beside ritual, astronomical, and devotional material in the miscellanies of a literate clerical world.

sources.label: Frank Klaassen, The Transformations of Magic
sources.abstract: This book argues that medieval magic banned by the church came in two flavors: "image magic" (making charms and talismans), which scholars could half-justify with the science of the day, and "ritual magic" (rituals for summoning spirits), which could not. Klaassen traces how interest in the first kind faded after 1500 while the second kept going and helped shape early modern magic.

scroll.definition: A *scroll* is a rolled manuscript: a continuous length of papyrus or parchment wound around a rod and read by unrolling. It is the format the bound *codex* displaced in late antiquity, and it survives in magic as the shape of the written charm — a strip of inscribed words worn on the body, buried, or held aloft in ritual.
