scroll.history: Middle English *scrowle*, an alteration of *scrowe*, 'a scrap or strip', reshaped under the influence of *rolle*, 'a roll'. The turn is that the scroll marks the losing side of a media war: the emperor Augustus is said to have burned two thousand magical scrolls in a single year, and the last rolls of Alexandria perished with the Serapeum. Yet the rolled strip kept its power in ritual — curse tablets were rolled up as scrolls, and amulet-scripts were worn against harm. The name endures in Magic's *Scroll* artifacts, and in the verb we now do to a glowing screen.

sources.label: Richard Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages
sources.abstract: A widely used introduction to what magic actually was in medieval Europe: who practiced it, what forms it took, and how it connected to religion, science, storytelling, and law. It also explains how fear of magic fed into the witch trials of later centuries.

sources.label: John G. Gager, Curse Tablets and Binding Spells
sources.abstract: Gager collects and translates ancient Greek and Roman "curse tablets" -- thin sheets of lead or other metal inscribed with spells asking gods or spirits to harm a rival, a thief, or a romantic competitor -- with commentary explaining how ordinary people, not just professional sorcerers, used these rituals in everyday disputes.

testament.definition: A *testament* is a witnessed writing — first a legal will, then, in Christian usage, either half of Scripture. To call the Bible's two parts the Old and New Testaments is to call them witnessed covenants between God and humanity, sworn and set down rather than merely told.
testament.history: From Latin *testamentum*, 'a will', built on *testis*, 'a witness' — the same root as *testify*. The turn is legal before it is scriptural: a testament is a covenant made valid by witnesses, and the Church borrowed the word to name God's two covenants, the New sealed, in the ritual formula, by 'my blood of the new testament'. The witnessing sense runs everywhere through learned magic, whose oaths, adjurations, and sworn books (the *Liber Iuratus*) bind spirits and readers alike by testimony.

sources.label: Richard Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages
sources.abstract: A widely used introduction to what magic actually was in medieval Europe: who practiced it, what forms it took, and how it connected to religion, science, storytelling, and law. It also explains how fear of magic fed into the witch trials of later centuries.

sources.label: Peterson & Harms, Book of Magic with Instructions for Invoking Spirits
sources.abstract: A modern transcription, by Joseph Peterson and Dan Harms, of a real handwritten spellbook from around 1577-1583, kept today at the Folger Shakespeare Library. It's an eclectic collection of spells and invocations with magic circles and drawn portraits of the spirits, angels, and demons it describes how to contact.

treatise.definition: A *treatise* is a formal, sustained written argument that handles a single subject in full. Where a *manuscript* names a book by how it was made and a *bestiary* by what it collects, a treatise is defined by its method — systematic exposition — and it is the characteristic form of learned magic, from Agrippa's three books to Thabit's *De Imaginibus*.
treatise.history: From Old French *traitis*, from Latin *tractare*, 'to handle, manage' (frequentative of *trahere*, 'to drag'). To write a treatise is, by the root, to 'handle' a topic thoroughly — the same *tractare* behind *treat*, *tract*, and *traction*. The turn is how the form dominates the magical archive: Agrippa's *De Occulta Philosophia* is the treatise that synthesized Renaissance magic, the *Picatrix* is a treatise on astral magic, and scholars measure such works by how much of the page the principal treatise occupies against its glosses.

sources.label: Attrell & Porreca, Picatrix: A Medieval Treatise on Astral Magic
sources.abstract: The first full English translation of a medieval how-to manual, originally written in Arabic and later translated into Latin, that teaches readers how to build magical charms, mix potions, and use astrology to draw on the supposed powers of the planets. It became one of the most influential sourcebooks for later European magic.

augury.definition: *Augury* is the reading of omens in the flight, feeding, and cries of birds. Where the *haruspex* consults entrails and the *hydromancer* a bowl of water, the augur watches the sky, judging whether the gods approve a proposed act rather than forecasting a fixed future.
augury.history: The word descends from Latin *augurium*, the divination by birds practiced by the college of *augures* who opened every Roman public act. Rome's elaborate norms did not survive the ancient world intact; as Kieckhefer notes, medieval Europe kept only a primitive sort of popular augury that read the future from the calls of birds, the number of a crow's croaks. Moralists then folded it into their catalogues of forbidden arts alongside soothsaying and the interpretation of dreams. The Roman ritual survives fossilized in the word *inauguration*.

sources.label: Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages
sources.abstract: A widely used introduction to what magic actually was in medieval Europe: who practiced it, what forms it took, and how it connected to religion, science, storytelling, and law. It also explains how fear of magic fed into the witch trials of later centuries.

sources.label: Clark, Thinking with Demons
sources.abstract: Looks at how educated Europeans between the 1400s and 1700s wrote about witches and demons, and argues these ideas were not fringe nonsense but fit logically with how those same thinkers understood nature, religion, history, and politics at the time.

auspice.definition: An *auspice* is a sign taken from birds, and the art of reading it — the Roman diviner's check on whether the gods favored an undertaking. Closely twinned with *augury*, it survives chiefly in the plural: to act 'under good auspices' is to begin when the omens are favorable.
auspice.history: Latin *auspicium* is literally bird-watching, from *avis* ('bird') plus *specere* ('to look'); the diviner who took it was the *auspex*. Roman teaching held, as one ancient source preserved in the magical record puts it, that neither evil omens nor any auspices affect those who at the outset declare that they take no notice of them — the omen bound only those who consented to it. The concrete bird-sign faded, but the sense of a favorable moment survives wherever a thing is done under someone's *auspices*.

sources.label: Mastrocinque et al., Ancient Magic: Then and Now
sources.abstract: This is a collection of essays by classical-studies scholars defending 'magic' as a useful, meaningful category for understanding the ancient world, rather than a vague or biased label. Contributors offer fresh interpretations of real magical objects from Greek and Roman antiquity, such as curse tablets, protective amulets, and written spell recipes.

clairvoyance.definition: *Clairvoyance* is the alleged faculty of perceiving hidden or distant things directly, without the senses or any instrument. It stands apart from the method-based arts of divination: where the *haruspex* needs entrails and the *geomancer* his figures, the clairvoyant claims simply to see clearly — a power of the seer, not a technique.
clairvoyance.history: The word is French — *clair* ('clear') and *voyance* ('seeing') — and, unlike its ancient cousins, it is young. It was coined by the mesmerists and spiritualists of the nineteenth century for the visions of entranced somnambulists; as Hanegraaff observes, subjects who believe in their visions come to think of their experiences as instances of clairvoyance, 'seeing clearly.' Earlier writers had the idea without the label — the Renaissance held that melancholy, separating soul from body, could bestow the gift of clairvoyance and premonition — but the term itself is the séance's own contribution to an ancient family of seeing-words.

sources.label: Hanegraaff, Hermetic Spirituality and the Historical Imagination
sources.abstract: Looks at the ancient Egyptian writings attributed to the legendary teacher Hermes Trismegistus and argues they weren't just abstract philosophy — they describe real spiritual exercises meant to shift a person's state of mind so they could see past ordinary illusions to a deeper truth about reality.

sources.label: Couliano, Eros and Magic in the Renaissance
sources.abstract: Couliano argues that Renaissance magic wasn't primitive superstition but an early, systematic theory of persuasion: thinkers like Ficino and Bruno believed a skilled operator could sway people's minds and emotions by understanding how desire and imagination work, making the Renaissance magician a distant ancestor of the modern advertiser or propagandist.

diviner.definition: A *diviner* is the practitioner of divination — the general name for anyone who seeks hidden knowledge through signs, lots, dreams, entrails, or spirits. The word covers the whole family the specialists divide up: the *haruspex*, the *augur*, the *geomancer*, and the dream-reader are all diviners, distinguished only by method.
diviner.history: The word comes from Latin *divinus*, 'of a god': to divine is to do the gods' own work, to know what is hidden from ordinary sight. Antiquity used a single broad term — the Greek *mantis*, as Christian Bull notes, was a general designation for a wide array of diviners, who could interpret signs such as the flight of birds, entrails, stars, omens or dreams. Medieval and Tudor England kept the word close to the ground in its 'cunning men,' the semi-professional healers and diviners who worked privately for a fee. The sense stayed literal longest in the water-*diviner*, still dowsing for what lies unseen beneath the earth.

sources.label: Bull, The Tradition of Hermes Trismegistus
sources.abstract: This book argues that the mystical writings credited to "Hermes Trismegistus" (a legendary wise teacher blending the Greek god Hermes with the Egyptian god Thoth) weren't just abstract philosophy, but grew out of real spiritual exercises and rituals practiced by small circles of Egyptian priests who combined their temple traditions with Greek philosophy.

sources.label: Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages
sources.abstract: A widely used introduction to what magic actually was in medieval Europe: who practiced it, what forms it took, and how it connected to religion, science, storytelling, and law. It also explains how fear of magic fed into the witch trials of later centuries.

geomancy.definition: *Geomancy* is divination by earth: the diviner generates a field of dots or points, at first by jabbing the ground at random, then reads the resulting figures through a fixed set of houses and meanings. Among the *-mancy* arts it is the most rule-bound and diagrammatic — a technical procedure closer to astrology's tables than to the entrail-gazing of the *haruspex*.
geomancy.history: The name joins Greek *gē* ('earth') to *manteia* ('divination'), but the developed art the Middle Ages knew was not Greek in origin. It entered Latin Europe from the Arabic *'ilm al-raml*, the 'science of sand,' and MedievalMagicDB records it as one of the most widely copied and debated of the specific divinatory arts — technical enough to circulate as a manual of figures and judgments, yet suspect enough to draw repeated condemnation. Skinner notes that of Isidore of Seville's tidy four elemental forms of divination, geomancy was the one that genuinely fit. The word survives in Magic as the *geomancer* and in the game's earthbound imagery.

sources.label: Skinner, Magical Techniques and Implements
sources.abstract: Skinner's doctoral thesis traces specific magic tools and step-by-step methods (like ritual instruments and spirit-summoning procedures) as they passed from ancient Greek-Egyptian magic texts, through Byzantine Greek manuscripts, into later European handbooks of magic — showing one continuous tradition rather than separate inventions.

haruspex.definition: A *haruspex* is a diviner who reads the will of the gods in the entrails of a sacrificed animal, above all the liver. Unlike the *augur*, who watches birds, or the *hydromancer*, who reads water, the *haruspex* works from the sacrificial body itself, matching the organ's marks against a schematized map of the divine order.
haruspex.history: The Latin *haruspex* is a 'gut-gazer' — *haru-* ('entrails') plus a root meaning 'observer.' The Romans, as one Renaissance source recalls, imported from Etruria this class of soothsayer, and the *haruspices* — the diviners who read entrails — remained influential enough that Firmicus Maternus listed them beside priests, augurs, and astrologers among the offices Mercury bestows. Reports of an earthquake sending the *haruspices* to name which god required appeasement show the art woven into the machinery of Roman public religion. Magic revives the office as a class of gut-reading seers.

sources.label: Ginzburg, Ecstasies: Deciphering the Witches' Sabbath
sources.abstract: Ginzburg pulls together witch-trial records, old sermons, folklore, and popular artwork from across Europe to argue that stories of witches flying to night gatherings echo a much older, widespread folk belief in spirit-journeys (a kind of trance travel found in shamanic traditions), rather than being straightforward evidence of devil-worship.

sources.label: Bull, The Tradition of Hermes Trismegistus
sources.abstract: This book argues that the mystical writings credited to "Hermes Trismegistus" (a legendary wise teacher blending the Greek god Hermes with the Egyptian god Thoth) weren't just abstract philosophy, but grew out of real spiritual exercises and rituals practiced by small circles of Egyptian priests who combined their temple traditions with Greek philosophy.

hydromancy.definition: *Hydromancy* is divination by water — reading omens or visions in a bowl, spring, or pool. Its receptive, reflective medium sets it apart from the earth-figures of *geomancy* or the fire of pyromancy, and its use of a still surface for seeing images makes it the direct ancestor of scrying.
hydromancy.history: The word pairs Greek *hydōr* ('water') with *manteia* ('divination'), and it recurs through medieval taxonomies of the forbidden arts, usually named in one breath with geomancy, chiromancy, and pyromancy. Water's moving, reflective, receptive qualities made it a potent interpretive medium; MedievalMagicDB notes that the art survives more as a category in condemnatory lists than in dedicated manuals. Its practical heart was seeing visions in water, and, as Harkness records of the Elizabethan scryers, this fed directly into crystal-gazing: the earliest methods of scrying concentrated upon aspects of hydromancy, the seeing of visions in water.

sources.label: Harkness, John Dee's Conversations with Angels
sources.abstract: Explores why the Elizabethan scholar John Dee spent his later years trying to talk to angels through a psychic medium, arguing these sessions grew naturally out of his earlier scientific and religious interests rather than being a sign he had lost his mind.

oneiromancy.definition: *Oneiromancy* is divination by dreams — the interpretation of what is seen in sleep as a message about waking events to come. Where most of the *-mancy* arts read an external medium, oneiromancy reads the diviner's own visions, treating the dream as a coded text to be decoded rather than a sign to be observed.
oneiromancy.history: The word joins Greek *oneiros* ('dream') to *manteia* ('divination'). Its founding text is Artemidorus' second-century *Oneirocritica*, the ancient world's great dream-dictionary, whose author, scholars note, presents himself not merely as an oneiromancer but as an expert in textual practices. The art never lost its hold: Kieckhefer records that in the medieval West the interpretation of dreams remained popular, as did treatises suggesting how to interpret them. Popular and learned dream-books circulated side by side, and the Enlightenment's mockers still had to list oneiromancy among the divinations they meant to bury.

sources.label: Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages
sources.abstract: A widely used introduction to what magic actually was in medieval Europe: who practiced it, what forms it took, and how it connected to religion, science, storytelling, and law. It also explains how fear of magic fed into the witch trials of later centuries.

sources.label: Artemidorus, Oneirocritica (via Edmonds et al., Magic and Religion in the Ancient Mediterranean)
sources.abstract: Written in the second century CE, this is the oldest surviving guide to interpreting dreams, cataloguing thousands of dream images and explaining what each was believed to mean for the dreamer's real life, treating dream interpretation as a practical skill much like fortune-telling.

portent.definition: A *portent* is a sign, especially an ominous one — an unusual event in the natural world read as a warning of things to come. Unlike the arts of divination, which the diviner performs, a portent is a sign received: it is not sought through a method but noticed and interpreted where it appears.
portent.history: Latin *portentum* comes from *portendere*, 'to foretell' — literally to stretch forward, so that the future reaches back to touch the present. Ancient and medieval writers read comets, monstrous births, and strange heavenly phenomena as portents; Isidore of Seville treated monsters as divine signs, usually a warning of catastrophe. As Keith Thomas describes, unusual happenings, or 'prodigies,' were taken as likely indications — portents — of judgements to come, a belief that lingered even as comets were absorbed into the new natural philosophy. Magic keeps the word for its omens and looming signs.

sources.label: Thomas, Religion and the Decline of Magic
sources.abstract: A landmark history of why belief in witchcraft, astrology, and protective charms was so common in 16th- and 17th-century England, and how the rise of Protestant religion and early science gradually pushed those beliefs to the margins of everyday life.

sources.label: Page, Magic in the Cloister
sources.abstract: A study of English monks who, despite their religious vows, collected and studied magic texts as part of their intellectual curiosity. It shows that for these monks, magic and monastic religious life weren't necessarily opposites — they could fit together.

premonition.definition: A premonition is a forewarning felt before the event — a sense of ill to come, arriving as a mood, a dream, or an uneasy sign rather than a reasoned forecast. Where *prescience* claims foreknowledge and *presage* reads outward omens, premonition is the inward, affective foretaste of what looms.
premonition.history: The root is Latin *praemonitio*, 'a forewarning', from *prae-* ('before') and *monēre* ('to warn') — the same warn-root that builds *monitor*, *admonish*, and *summon*. The word's magical career runs through the melancholic and the ominous: Renaissance writers held that melancholy, a kind of separation of soul from body, bestowed 'the gift of clairvoyance and premonition' (Couliano, *Eros and Magic*), while demonologists read wonders and monsters as premonitory signs of God's wrath (Clark, *Thinking with Demons*). Keith Thomas records the era's premonitory dreams, taken as almost certainly warnings of things to come.

sources.label: Ioan P. Couliano, Eros and Magic in the Renaissance
sources.abstract: Couliano argues that Renaissance magic wasn't primitive superstition but an early, systematic theory of persuasion: thinkers like Ficino and Bruno believed a skilled operator could sway people's minds and emotions by understanding how desire and imagination work, making the Renaissance magician a distant ancestor of the modern advertiser or propagandist.

sources.abstract: A landmark history of why belief in witchcraft, astrology, and protective charms was so common in 16th- and 17th-century England, and how the rise of Protestant religion and early science gradually pushed those beliefs to the margins of everyday life.

presage.definition: To presage is to portend — for a sign or event to point ahead to what will follow; as a noun, a presage is that foreboding sign itself. Unlike the inward, felt *premonition*, a presage is read outward, in omens and portents; unlike the neutral *prognostic*, it leans toward warning and dread.
presage.history: The word comes from Latin *praesagium*, 'a foreboding', from *prae-* ('before') and *sagire*, 'to perceive keenly' — the same keen-scenting root behind *sagacious*. In the literature of magic it names the omen that reaches forward: an eclipse that presaged the Sultan's death in John Dee's world (Parry, *The Arch Conjuror of England*), or the trivial occurrences God might send 'to presage success or failure' (Thomas, *Religion and the Decline of Magic*). To demonologists, the very flourishing of witches was 'a presage of the coming desolation' of the faith (Clark, *Thinking with Demons*).

sources.label: Glyn Parry, The Arch Conjuror of England: John Dee
sources.abstract: A biography arguing that John Dee was not just an eccentric magician on the sidelines of history, but a real political operator whose magic and angel-conversations were tangled up with Elizabethan court intrigue and Europe's religious conflicts.

sources.label: Keith Thomas, Religion and the Decline of Magic
sources.abstract: A landmark history of why belief in witchcraft, astrology, and protective charms was so common in 16th- and 17th-century England, and how the rise of Protestant religion and early science gradually pushed those beliefs to the margins of everyday life.

sources.label: Stuart Clark, Thinking with Demons
sources.abstract: Looks at how educated Europeans between the 1400s and 1700s wrote about witches and demons, and argues these ideas were not fringe nonsense but fit logically with how those same thinkers understood nature, religion, history, and politics at the time.

prescience.definition: Prescience is foreknowledge — knowing a thing before it happens, held as a settled state rather than a fleeting sign. Where a *premonition* is felt and a *presage* is read, prescience is possessed, and in theological debate it was the very faculty that only God fully owned.
prescience.history: The word is Latin *praescientia*, 'foreknowledge', from *prae-* ('before') and *scire*, 'to know' — literally a kind of 'fore-science', the same *scire* beneath *science* itself. Renaissance thinkers glossed it exactly so: Pico della Mirandola treated prescience as 'knowledge of the future, encompassed by the terms *praenotio*, *prognosis*' (Akopyan, *Debating the Stars*). The magical stakes were high — demonologists insisted the devil could not compete in prescience with God (Clark, *Thinking with Demons*), and Keith Thomas records claims of miraculous prescience among the godly. The word thus hovered between science, gift, and forbidden overreach.

sources.label: Ovanes Akopyan, Debating the Stars in the Italian Renaissance
sources.abstract: Akopyan traces the argument that broke out after the philosopher Pico della Mirandola wrote a book in the 1490s attacking astrology, the belief that the positions of stars and planets control events on Earth. The book follows how supporters and critics across Italy and Europe fought over Pico's ideas for decades after his death.

sources.label: Stuart Clark, Thinking with Demons

sources.abstract: A landmark history of why belief in witchcraft, astrology, and protective charms was so common in 16th- and 17th-century England, and how the rise of Protestant religion and early science gradually pushed those beliefs to the margins of everyday life.

prognostic.definition: A prognostic is a forecast drawn from signs — a reasoned prediction of an outcome, whether of an illness, a harvest, or the weather. More clinical than the ominous *presage* and less absolute than *prescience*, it carries the flavor of method and diagnosis: the practitioner reads present indications to say what will come.
prognostic.history: The word descends from Greek *prognōstikon*, 'a foreknowing', from *pro-* ('before') and *gignōskein* ('to know'), and it entered the West through Hippocratic medicine, where a *Prognostic* judged whether a patient would live or die. That bedside sense clung on: physicians and wizards alike made 'both diagnosis and prognostication on the basis of the patient's urine' (Thomas, *Religion and the Decline of Magic*), and the Greek magical papyri preserve a 'prognostic of life and death' (Betz, *Greek Magical Papyri*). From there it drifted into astrology and the almanac — the prognostications of flood and fortune that fill Renaissance libraries (Klaassen, *The Transformations of Magic*).

sources.label: Keith Thomas, Religion and the Decline of Magic
sources.abstract: A landmark history of why belief in witchcraft, astrology, and protective charms was so common in 16th- and 17th-century England, and how the rise of Protestant religion and early science gradually pushed those beliefs to the margins of everyday life.

sources.label: Frank Klaassen, The Transformations of Magic
sources.abstract: This book argues that medieval magic banned by the church came in two flavors: "image magic" (making charms and talismans), which scholars could half-justify with the science of the day, and "ritual magic" (rituals for summoning spirits), which could not. Klaassen traces how interest in the first kind faded after 1500 while the second kept going and helped shape early modern magic.

sources.label: Hans Dieter Betz, The Greek Magical Papyri in Translation
sources.abstract: A translated collection of real spells, charms, and magical recipes written on papyrus in Greco-Roman Egypt roughly 1,600 to 2,200 years ago, giving readers direct access to what ordinary magical practice actually looked like rather than what critics said about it.

prophesy.definition: To prophesy is to declare a divine message — to speak, with authority not one's own, of what a god intends, which often means what is to come. It is a foretelling-word rooted in speech, not sight: the prophet is a mouthpiece, set apart from the omen-readers and sign-watchers of the divination family.
prophesy.history: A prophet speaks FOR a god — Greek *prophētēs*, from *pro-* ('before, for') and *phēnai* ('to speak') — and thus, often, before the event. In medieval learning the word sat uneasily beside the forbidden arts: Petrus Alfonsi records a dispute over whether the seventh liberal art was prophecy or necromancy (Klaassen, *The Transformations of Magic*), and grimoires still promised the operator 'the Spirit of Prophesy' (*The Lesser Key of Solomon*). The later English tidy-up split the spelling — *prophecy* for the noun, *prophesy* for the verb — a distinction Scripture's own cadence long resisted.

sources.label: Frank Klaassen, The Transformations of Magic
sources.abstract: This book argues that medieval magic banned by the church came in two flavors: "image magic" (making charms and talismans), which scholars could half-justify with the science of the day, and "ritual magic" (rituals for summoning spirits), which could not. Klaassen traces how interest in the first kind faded after 1500 while the second kept going and helped shape early modern magic.

sources.label: Joseph H. Peterson (ed.), The Lesser Key of Solomon
sources.abstract: A modern scholarly edition of a 17th-century handbook of magic (called a "grimoire") that lists methods and diagrams for summoning and commanding spirits, put together and corrected by Peterson from several old manuscript copies held in the British Library.

pyromancy.definition: Pyromancy is divination by fire — reading the future in flame, its color, its crackle, and the shapes it throws. One of a long roster of named mantic arts, it takes its place beside geomancy (earth), hydromancy (water), and aeromancy (air) in the classical scheme that sorts prophecy by its element.
pyromancy.history: The name pairs Greek *pyr*, 'fire', with *manteia*, 'divination' — the same suffix-machine that mass-produces the mantic arts. Fire, alive and unpredictable, made an old oracle, and medieval and Renaissance writers routinely listed pyromancy among the technical forms of divination — geomancy, hydromancy, pyromancy, aeromancy — condemned by the casuists (Clark, *Thinking with Demons*); Pico swept it up among the 'inanities' of superstition (Farmer, *Pico's 900 Theses*). MedievalMagicDB classes it a term for divination by fire, recurring in the lists of illicit knowledge — a category whose weight, like its sister arts, came as much from enumeration as from any surviving manual.

sources.label: Stuart Clark, Thinking with Demons
sources.abstract: Looks at how educated Europeans between the 1400s and 1700s wrote about witches and demons, and argues these ideas were not fringe nonsense but fit logically with how those same thinkers understood nature, religion, history, and politics at the time.

sources.label: Stephen A. Farmer, Giovanni Pico della Mirandola: Syncretism in the West
sources.abstract: This book presents a full translation and study of Pico della Mirandola's famous 900 Theses of 1486, in which the young philosopher tried to show that Christian, Jewish, Islamic, and ancient Greek and Roman ideas about the universe could all be combined into one grand system of thought.

seer.definition: A seer is one who sees the hidden — a beholder of visions and futures. It is the plainest word in the whole divination family, describing the diviner by the bare act of sight; where a *sibyl* is a titled prophetess and a *soothsayer* a truth-teller, the seer simply, and homely, sees.
seer.history: The word is homegrown: Old English *sēon*, 'to see', plus the agent ending *-er*, standing among a crowd of borrowed Greco-Latin *-mancers*. Its sense is the oldest kind of knowing — an Irish tale calls its seer a *fissid*, 'one who knows' (Page and Rider, *The Routledge History of Medieval Magic*), and myth gave the gift most memorably to Tiresias, the blind seer (Ginzburg, *Ecstasies*). In the crystal-gazing of the Renaissance the seer became a working role: John Dee begged God for 'a good seer, and skryer of spirituall apparitions' (Page, *Magic in the Cloister*), the one who watched the glass while the magician read the book.

sources.label: Sophie Page & Catherine Rider (eds.), The Routledge History of Medieval Magic
sources.abstract: A large reference collection that rounds up current scholarship on magic in medieval Europe (roughly 1100 to 1500), covering the texts people used, how the church and universities reacted, and how magic touched gender roles, art, medicine, and the early witch trials.

sources.label: Sophie Page, Magic in the Cloister
sources.abstract: A study of English monks who, despite their religious vows, collected and studied magic texts as part of their intellectual curiosity. It shows that for these monks, magic and monastic religious life weren't necessarily opposites — they could fit together.

sources.label: Carlo Ginzburg, Ecstasies: Deciphering the Witches' Sabbath
sources.abstract: Ginzburg pulls together witch-trial records, old sermons, folklore, and popular artwork from across Europe to argue that stories of witches flying to night gatherings echo a much older, widespread folk belief in spirit-journeys (a kind of trance travel found in shamanic traditions), rather than being straightforward evidence of devil-worship.

sibyl.definition: A sibyl is a prophetess of antiquity — one of the inspired women who uttered oracles in a divine frenzy, most famously the Cumaean Sibyl who guided Aeneas to the underworld. Where a *seer* merely sees and a *soothsayer* speaks truth, the sibyl prophesies possessed, her scattered utterances gathered into books and consulted for centuries.
sibyl.history: The word comes through Latin *sibylla* from Greek *síbylla*, 'prophetess'. Antiquity's freelance oracles, the Sibyls left behind the Sibylline books — so prized that when Tarquin burned all but three, Rome paid a fortune for what remained (Reuchlin, *Augenspiegel*). Their great turn was Christian: Lactantius grouped 'Hermes with the Sibyl' among the pagan witnesses to Christ (Litwa, *Hermetica II*), and so the Sibyls were carved beside the prophets in cathedral choir-stalls and floor-mosaics — pagan prophetesses folded into the church's own prehistory (Kieckhefer, *Magic in the Middle Ages*).

sources.label: Richard Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages
sources.abstract: A widely used introduction to what magic actually was in medieval Europe: who practiced it, what forms it took, and how it connected to religion, science, storytelling, and law. It also explains how fear of magic fed into the witch trials of later centuries.

sources.label: M. David Litwa, Hermetica II
sources.abstract: Litwa translates a scattered set of ancient writings credited to Hermes Trismegistus, a legendary wise teacher who blended Egyptian and Greek ideas about God and the cosmos. The book rounds out an earlier translated collection of these "Hermetic" texts, which ancient readers used for spiritual philosophy and which later Renaissance magicians mined for ideas.

sources.label: Johannes Reuchlin, Augenspiegel (study)
sources.abstract: In 1511 the German scholar Johannes Reuchlin published this pamphlet to argue against a campaign to burn all Jewish religious books, risking his own reputation to defend Jewish scholarship. This Brill edition is the first full modern English translation, with notes explaining the controversy and why it mattered for early debates about tolerance and free inquiry.

soothsayer.definition: A soothsayer is a truth-teller who foretells — one who declares what is hidden or to come, whether by dream, sign, or spirit. The word is a foreteller's title like *seer* and *sibyl*, but it names the diviner by the claim being made: to speak sooth, the truth, about the future.
soothsayer.history: A soothsayer is a 'truth-sayer', from Old English *sōth*, 'truth', and *secgan*, 'to say' — and it is now the last hiding place of *sooth*, a word vanished from English but for this and *forsooth*. The compound long served as the plain vernacular for the diviner the church condemned: English Bibles set it among 'your prophets, and diviners, and dreamers... and soothsayers, and sorcerers' (Jeremiah 27:9, in Fanger, *Rewriting Magic*), and Ammianus recorded that under Rome merely consulting a soothsayer could bring capital punishment (Kieckhefer, *Magic in the Middle Ages*). The Greek behind such passages is often *mantis*, the seer-healer.

sources.label: Claire Fanger, Rewriting Magic
sources.abstract: A close study of a 14th-century French monk's own written account of creating a prayer ritual he believed could instantly grant knowledge — a project that got his book publicly condemned and burned as heresy in 1323. Fanger uses his firsthand story to explore how medieval people justified using something so close to forbidden magic.

sources.label: Richard Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages
sources.abstract: A widely used introduction to what magic actually was in medieval Europe: who practiced it, what forms it took, and how it connected to religion, science, storytelling, and law. It also explains how fear of magic fed into the witch trials of later centuries.

conjurer.definition: A conjurer summons spirits and binds them to obedience, commanding demons or angels to appear within a circle and do his bidding. Where a *necromancer* questions the dead and a *diviner* reads signs, the conjurer compels a present spirit face to face. By the 1500s the same word also named the stage-trickster who only seems to.
conjurer.history: The word comes from Latin *coniurare*, 'to swear together' — a conspiracy of oaths. Ritual magic kept that oath-logic literal: the conjurer binds a spirit not by force but by adjuration, swearing it into compliance with divine names, exactly as an exorcist commands a demon. Kieckhefer notes the conjurer's stance was at once coercive and petitionary, half-command, half-prayer. The turn came in the sixteenth century, when 'conjurer' split: Reginald Scot could call the pope an 'arch-conjurer,' while the same word slid toward the fairground illusionist. Both senses survive, and Magic uses the older one — a caller of spirits.

sources.label: Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages
sources.abstract: A widely used introduction to what magic actually was in medieval Europe: who practiced it, what forms it took, and how it connected to religion, science, storytelling, and law. It also explains how fear of magic fed into the witch trials of later centuries.

sources.label: Klaassen, The Transformations of Magic
sources.abstract: This book argues that medieval magic banned by the church came in two flavors: "image magic" (making charms and talismans), which scholars could half-justify with the science of the day, and "ritual magic" (rituals for summoning spirits), which could not. Klaassen traces how interest in the first kind faded after 1500 while the second kept going and helped shape early modern magic.

enchanter.definition: An enchanter works magic by the spoken or sung word — chanting spells that charm, bind, or transform. He differs from a conjurer, who summons spirits to do his will: the enchanter's power is in the incantation itself, the utterance that fascinates its hearer or reshapes the world. The feminine *enchantress* names the same art.
enchanter.history: The word descends from Latin *incantare*, 'to sing a spell upon' (via Old French *enchanteur*) — literally a singer over things. The sung root stayed audible: the historian Claire Fanger even titled an essay 'Things Done Wisely by a Wise Enchanter' — about the very power that spoken words were once believed to hold, the same power this word has always named. The defining turn was literary rather than lexical. Geoffrey of Monmouth's twelfth-century Merlin became, as the *Routledge History of Medieval Magic* observes, the model for most later male enchanters, fixing the figure as a worker of transformative wonders. The word lands as both a folk charmer named in trial records and the romance sorcerer — a caster who sings.

sources.label: Fanger, 'Things Done Wisely by a Wise Enchanter'
sources.abstract: A scholarly article on a strange medieval debate: could spoken words alone have real power? The author shows that some 13th-century thinkers, like Roger Bacon, argued certain words could work through natural (almost proto-scientific) causes, while others insisted that any word-magic that actually worked had to be secretly powered by demons.

sources.label: Page & Rider, The Routledge History of Medieval Magic
sources.abstract: A large reference collection that rounds up current scholarship on magic in medieval Europe (roughly 1100 to 1500), covering the texts people used, how the church and universities reacted, and how magic touched gender roles, art, medicine, and the early witch trials.

exorcist.definition: An exorcist expels or restrains a demon by invoking a higher authority — driving out an unclean spirit through sacred names and consecrated formulae. He is the conjurer's mirror image: both command spirits by adjuration, but the exorcist casts out where the conjurer calls in, and the Church authorizes his rite as clerical office rather than illicit art.
exorcist.history: The word comes from Greek *exorkistēs*, from *exorkizein*, 'to bind by oath' (*ex-* plus *horkos*, 'oath'). The logic is that a demon is not overpowered but out-sworn — bound by a stronger oath in a stronger name. Kieckhefer notes Christian exorcists could command demons by using powerful and secret names, the name of Jesus foremost. The recurring turn was policing the boundary: as Trithemius insisted, the exorcist vested with power to expunge demons had to be told apart from the demon-conjuring sorcerer whose verbal technology looked almost identical. The word lands squarely on that contested line between sacrament and spell.

sources.label: Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages
sources.abstract: A widely used introduction to what magic actually was in medieval Europe: who practiced it, what forms it took, and how it connected to religion, science, storytelling, and law. It also explains how fear of magic fed into the witch trials of later centuries.

sources.label: Brann, Trithemius and Magical Theology
sources.abstract: Brann examines Johannes Trithemius, a Benedictine abbot who tried to argue that certain forms of magic were compatible with, and even useful to, Christian faith, and shows how this claim triggered a long-running controversy over the occult that ran through the Reformation and into the rise of modern science.

hierophant.definition: A hierophant reveals sacred things — the presiding priest who displays the holy objects and interprets the mystery to initiates. Unlike a diviner, who extracts hidden knowledge from signs, the hierophant guards knowledge already revealed and admits others into it. His office is disclosure under authority, not prediction.
hierophant.history: The word is Greek *hierophantēs*, 'revealer of holy things' (*hieros*, 'sacred,' plus *phainein*, 'to show') — the chief priest of the Eleusinian Mysteries who showed the sacred objects to the initiated. The turn came through Neoplatonism: as Szönyi shows, later philosophy turned the philosopher into a hierophant, recasting the priest of ritual disclosure as the sage who unveils divine reality, and Renaissance thinkers linked the word to the manifestation of the sacred. It lands as a title for the master of initiation — and, in the Tarot, the fifth trump.

sources.label: Szönyi, John Dee's Occultism
sources.abstract: Argues that John Dee's wide-ranging interests in science, mathematics, and angel-summoning were all held together by one goal: his belief that humans could spiritually elevate themselves toward a god-like state through hidden knowledge and symbols.

magus.definition: A magus is a master magician of the loftiest, most learned kind — the philosopher-mage who reads the hidden correspondences of the cosmos and works within them. Where an ordinary conjurer or enchanter performs an operation, the magus commands a whole worldview, mediating between the terrestrial and the celestial. The word keeps the hieratic dignity of the Persian priest-astrologer.
magus.history: The word is Latin *magus*, from Greek *magos*, from Old Persian *maguš* — originally a Persian priest and astrologer, as the Renaissance well knew, though the claim that *magus* simply means 'wise man' in Persian is a repeated commonplace more than a verified fact. Its ambivalence was fixed early: the New Testament sets the wonder-working Simon Magus against the apostles, making 'magus' a name for the false wonder-worker as readily as the wise one. The rehabilitating turn came with Ficino, Pico, and Agrippa, whom scholars like Frances Yates cast as the exalted 'Renaissance Magus' — a co-creator ordering nature by knowledge. Magic uses the word for its most elevated practitioners.

sources.label: Paola Zambelli, Astrology and Magic from the Medieval Latin and Islamic World to Renaissance Europe
sources.abstract: A collection of the author's essays tracing how astrology (reading the stars to predict events) passed from Islamic and Latin medieval scholars into the Renaissance, and how astrologers actually worked as court advisors and public intellectuals, not just armchair theorists.

sources.label: Yates, Giordano Bruno and the Hermetic Tradition
sources.abstract: A classic study arguing that the Renaissance philosopher Giordano Bruno, burned at the stake in 1600, was driven less by early modern science than by "Hermeticism" -- a set of ancient-seeming magical and mystical teachings he believed held the secret key to controlling nature.

sources.label: Etymonline, s.v. magus (Greek magos ← Old Persian maguš)

occultist.definition: An occultist studies and practices the hidden arts — the 'occult sciences' of astrology, magic, and spirit-communication pursued as a unified body of secret knowledge. The term is deliberately broad where magus, conjurer, or diviner are specific: an occultist is defined less by a single technique than by a commitment to what lies concealed from ordinary sight.
occultist.history: The word builds on Latin *occultus*, 'hidden,' with the agent-suffix *-ist* — but the practitioner-noun is a modern coinage, arriving in the nineteenth century alongside Spiritualism and the revival of ceremonial magic. The medieval and Renaissance materials it retrospectively organizes knew no such job title: they spoke of the *magus*, of *philosophia occulta* — the 'hidden philosophy' Agrippa systematized — and of specific arts. The turn is precisely that Victorian moment when the scattered 'occult sciences' were gathered under one name and one self-conscious identity. The word lands as the title for the modern esotericist.

thaumaturge.definition: A thaumaturge is a worker of wonders — one who produces marvels and miracles, whether by holiness or by art. The word describes an effect rather than a method: where a conjurer names his means (summoning spirits) and a diviner his end (hidden knowledge), the thaumaturge is known simply by the astonishing result. It was applied first to miracle-working saints, then to magicians.
thaumaturge.history: The word is Greek *thaumatourgos*, 'wonder-worker' (*thauma*, 'wonder,' plus *ergon*, 'work') — the same *ergon* hidden in *theurgy* and *energy*. It attached first to holy marvel-workers: Byzantine hagiography celebrates figures like St. Theodore of Sykeon as thaumaturges. The turn was its migration from sanctity into the suspect. By the Renaissance, John Dee could dismiss 'thaumaturgy' or mere 'wonder-working' as illicit ritual that did not draw its power from true divinity — even while listing thaumaturgy among the mathematical arts. The word lands ambivalent, poised between the saint's miracle and the conjurer's trick.

sources.label: Håkansson, Seeing the Word: John Dee and Renaissance Occultism
sources.abstract: A doctoral thesis arguing that John Dee's strange mix of interests -- grammar, the Kabbalah, astronomy, alchemy, and math -- were not scattered hobbies but one connected project: recovering a lost perfect language Dee believed could unlock both nature's secrets and God's truth.

sources.label: Meyer & Mirecki, Magic and Ritual in the Ancient World
sources.abstract: This edited collection of scholarly essays asks what counted as "magic" (as opposed to accepted religion) across the ancient Near East, Greece, Rome, Judaism, and early Christianity and Islam, using real spell texts, amulets, and rituals to explore where societies drew that line and why.

chthonic.definition: *Chthonic* describes what belongs to the earth and the underworld — its gods, powers, and rites, as against the bright sky-gods above. Greek religion drew a working line between the chthonic deities (Hades, Persephone, Demeter, Hekate) and the celestial Olympians; the word marks the darker, subterranean half of that divide.
chthonic.history: From Greek *chthonios*, 'of the earth, underworld' (from *chthōn*, 'earth'). Greek and later magical practice split the divine into two registers: the celestial gods above, and the chthonic powers below, invoked for curses, binding spells, and dealings with the dead. Curse tablets and the Greek magical papyri routinely summon chthonic *daimones* — the underworld gods a magician addressed by night, often through the grave of one who died young. Ancient writers cast such rites as the shadow-side of state religion, appeals to the powers under the ground rather than the august sky.

sources.label: Betz, The Greek Magical Papyri in Translation
sources.abstract: A translated collection of real spells, charms, and magical recipes written on papyrus in Greco-Roman Egypt roughly 1,600 to 2,200 years ago, giving readers direct access to what ordinary magical practice actually looked like rather than what critics said about it.

sources.label: Gager, Curse Tablets and Binding Spells from the Ancient World
sources.abstract: A translated collection of ancient Greek and Roman curse tablets -- small inscribed objects people buried or dedicated to gods to harm a rival, win a court case, or wreck a love affair -- showing how ordinary people used magic to handle everyday conflicts.

diabolic.definition: *Diabolic* means 'of the devil' — pertaining to Satan and his works, or to evil of a deliberate, malicious kind. In the history of magic it is the label that recast ordinary rites as demonic: a charm or cure becomes diabolic the moment authorities read a demon behind it.
diabolic.history: From Greek *diabolos*, 'slanderer, accuser' (from *dia-*, 'across,' + *ballein*, 'to throw' — one who throws accusations), which named the Devil in Greek scripture; 'devil' is simply *diabolos* worn down. The Devil is, by his own title, the prosecutor. The word's decisive turn came in the later Middle Ages, when theologians and inquisitors folded folk magic, healing, and learned ritual into a single story of diabolical pact — the process, as the historian Michael Bailey traces it, of constructing witchcraft as an accusation. To call an act diabolic was less to describe it than to convict it.

sources.label: David J. Collins, S.J. (ed.), The Sacred and the Sinister: Studies in Medieval Religion and Magic
sources.abstract: A collection of essays exploring how medieval people tried to draw a line between approved religious practice and forbidden magic — and how blurry and contested that line actually was in everyday life.

sources.label: Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages
sources.abstract: A widely used introduction to what magic actually was in medieval Europe: who practiced it, what forms it took, and how it connected to religion, science, storytelling, and law. It also explains how fear of magic fed into the witch trials of later centuries.

eldritch.definition: *Eldritch* means weird, uncanny, otherworldly — belonging to a realm outside the ordinary and slightly wrong for it. It carries a flavor no synonym quite matches: not merely strange but strange as if from elsewhere, a thing that does not sit right in this world.
eldritch.history: Probably from Old English *el-*, 'foreign, other,' plus *rīce*, 'realm, kingdom' — literally 'of the otherworldly kingdom.' The word survived chiefly in Scots and northern English, a quiet regional relic for the eerie and the elf-touched. Its defining turn came in the 20th century, when H. P. Lovecraft seized on *eldritch* as a keyword of cosmic horror and fixed its modern sense for good. It reaches Magic: The Gathering — in the Eldrazi and the set Eldritch Moon — already soaked in that Lovecraftian charge.

esoteric.definition: *Esoteric* describes knowledge meant for an inner circle — the initiated, the few — as opposed to what is taught openly. It is the word for the members-only teaching, guarded by secrecy and reserved for those judged worthy, in contrast to the *exoteric* doctrine offered to the public.
esoteric.history: From Greek *esōterikos*, 'inner' (from *esō*, 'within'). The distinction is old: ancient tradition split Aristotle's teaching into esoteric doctrines for students inside the school and exoteric works for outsiders. The magical and mystical traditions made the word their own — Jewish esoteric tradition from Merkabah throne-mysticism to Kabbalah, and Renaissance magi like John Dee addressing only the *mystae* and *initiati* deemed fit for hidden disciplines. Its landing in modern usage, 'Western esotericism,' names that whole current of guarded, initiatory knowledge.

sources.label: Håkansson, Seeing the Word: John Dee and Renaissance Occultism
sources.abstract: A doctoral thesis arguing that John Dee's strange mix of interests -- grammar, the Kabbalah, astronomy, alchemy, and math -- were not scattered hobbies but one connected project: recovering a lost perfect language Dee believed could unlock both nature's secrets and God's truth.

infernal.definition: *Infernal* means 'of the lower place' — belonging to hell and the underworld, or to its demons. It names the realm beneath, and by extension the spirits reached there: the infernal powers a magician conjured, as opposed to the celestial or aerial ones.
infernal.history: From Latin *infernus*, 'lower, underground' (from *inferus*, 'below') — hell is, etymologically, just 'the downstairs.' The *Hermetica* keep the sense literal, calling the underworld regions infernal because they are at the bottom of the sphere. Ritual texts sorted spirits by element and station — celestial, aerial, aquatic, and infernal — the last bound to perpetual flame in the *Picatrix*. Dante's *Inferno* then fixed the fire and geography of hell in the Western imagination, so that 'infernal' now means blazing as much as low.

sources.label: Copenhaver, Hermetica
sources.abstract: A fresh English translation of the Hermetica, a set of mystical Greek and Latin texts from ancient Egypt that Renaissance readers mistakenly believed were far older wisdom from a legendary sage named Hermes Trismegistus, with an introduction placing the texts in their real historical setting.

sources.label: Attrell & Porreca, Picatrix: A Medieval Treatise on Astral Magic
sources.abstract: The first full English translation of a medieval how-to manual, originally written in Arabic and later translated into Latin, that teaches readers how to build magical charms, mix potions, and use astrology to draw on the supposed powers of the planets. It became one of the most influential sourcebooks for later European magic.

cauldron.definition: A cauldron is a large pot for boiling over a fire — in magic, the vessel where a brew, potion, or drug is compounded. It is the witch's working vessel, and it contrasts with vessels of containment: where a *reliquary* holds and a *chalice* offers, the cauldron *transforms*, cooking raw materials into something new.
cauldron.history: From Latin *caldaria*, 'hot bath' (from *calidus*, 'hot'), by way of Old North French *caudron* — by origin merely a big pot for heating water, a domestic object made sinister by association. Classical myth already set it working: Medea brews her rejuvenating drug in a cauldron, and Circe her potions. Northern European witch imagery then sealed its fate, gathering women around the pot in print after print, until Shakespeare's weird sisters — 'Round about the cauldron go' — made it the fixed emblem of the witch's craft.

sources.label: Petropoulos, Greek Magic: Ancient, Medieval and Modern
sources.abstract: A collection of essays by different scholars tracing how Greek magic, including spells, curses, and protective charms, changed and stayed the same across three time periods: ancient Greece, the medieval Byzantine world, and modern Greek folk practice.

sources.label: Zika, The Appearance of Witchcraft
sources.abstract: Zika studies printed woodcuts and illustrated pamphlets from 16th-century Europe to show how artists invented and spread the now-familiar image of the witch (flying, naked, cooking over a cauldron), arguing these pictures shaped public fear of witchcraft as much as trial records or sermons did.

censer.definition: A censer is a vessel for burning incense — a pierced or open container in which fragrant matter is set smoking during rite. Its job is the smoke itself: where a *cauldron* boils and a *chalice* holds drink, the censer perfumes and purifies the air, carrying the *suffumigation* that consecrates a space or summons a spirit.
censer.history: From Latin *incensum*, 'that which is burnt,' via Old French *encensier* — the same root as *incense*, and as being *incensed* (inflamed) with anger. In both church and conjuring the censer did identical work: the priest censed the altar, and the magician suffumigated his circle. Ritual manuals from the Greek magical papyri through the *Picatrix* and the Solomonic grimoires treat it as standard equipment — the magician suffumigates the circle with a censer as smoke rises and the spirits are called.

sources.label: Klaassen, The Transformations of Magic
sources.abstract: This book argues that medieval magic banned by the church came in two flavors: "image magic" (making charms and talismans), which scholars could half-justify with the science of the day, and "ritual magic" (rituals for summoning spirits), which could not. Klaassen traces how interest in the first kind faded after 1500 while the second kept going and helped shape early modern magic.

sources.label: Attrell & Porreca, Picatrix: A Medieval Treatise on Astral Magic
sources.abstract: The first full English translation of a medieval how-to manual, originally written in Arabic and later translated into Latin, that teaches readers how to build magical charms, mix potions, and use astrology to draw on the supposed powers of the planets. It became one of the most influential sourcebooks for later European magic.

chalice.definition: A chalice is a ritual cup, above all the vessel that holds the wine of the Eucharist. It is a vessel of offering and communion — distinct from the *cauldron* that brews and the *censer* that burns; where they transform or perfume, the chalice presents a sacred drink to be shared or consecrated.
chalice.history: From Latin *calix*, 'cup' — a doublet of *calyx*, a flower's cup. Its charge is liturgical: the priest's chalice held the consecrated wine, the chalice of the new covenant, an object of such potency that not a drop could be spilled. From there the vessel rose into legend. Kieckhefer notes that *The Quest of the Holy Grail* takes the wonder-working chalice of the Last Supper as its central motif — the cup, the *calyx*, the chalice, and the Grail all one shape raised by degrees toward the sacred.

sources.label: Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages
sources.abstract: A widely used introduction to what magic actually was in medieval Europe: who practiced it, what forms it took, and how it connected to religion, science, storytelling, and law. It also explains how fear of magic fed into the witch trials of later centuries.

sources.label: Thomas, Religion and the Decline of Magic
sources.abstract: A landmark history of why belief in witchcraft, astrology, and protective charms was so common in 16th- and 17th-century England, and how the rise of Protestant religion and early science gradually pushed those beliefs to the margins of everyday life.

crystal-ball.definition: A crystal ball is a sphere of clear quartz or glass used for scrying — gazing into a reflective surface to see visions, spirits, or hidden truths. It belongs with the mirror, the polished stone, and the bowl of water as a scrying tool; what sets it apart is its form, a globe of transparent stone the seer looks *into* rather than upon.
crystal-ball.history: From Greek *krystallos*, 'ice, rock-crystal' (related to *kryos*, 'frost') — the Greeks held clear quartz to be ice frozen so hard it could never melt, so the scryer gazes into a ball of eternal ice. All scrying needed was a reflective surface — a mirror, a gem, even a thumbnail. The practice's most documented moment is Elizabethan: John Dee owned crystal globes and a 'shew-stone,' and through his skryer Edward Kelley claimed to summon angels into the crystal — the best-recorded divinatory sessions in the record.

sources.label: Szönyi, John Dee's Occultism
sources.abstract: Argues that John Dee's wide-ranging interests in science, mathematics, and angel-summoning were all held together by one goal: his belief that humans could spiritually elevate themselves toward a god-like state through hidden knowledge and symbols.

sources.label: Thomas, Religion and the Decline of Magic
sources.abstract: A landmark history of why belief in witchcraft, astrology, and protective charms was so common in 16th- and 17th-century England, and how the rise of Protestant religion and early science gradually pushed those beliefs to the margins of everyday life.

fetish.definition: A fetish is a made object believed to house or channel power — a crafted thing venerated for the spirit or force within it. It differs from a worn *amulet* or *talisman* in the weight of belief it carries: the fetish is not merely charged but treated as itself potent, an object inhabited by power.
fetish.history: From Portuguese *feitiço*, 'charm,' from Latin *facticius*, 'made by art' (from *facere*, 'to make'). Portuguese sailors coined the term for the West African power-objects they encountered, naming them for their made-ness. The word then traveled through European thought, each discipline borrowing it in turn — anthropology for ritual objects, Marx for the commodity, Freud for desire — so that European writers even applied it back onto their own past, calling holy relics wonder-working fetishes.

sources.label: Hanegraaff, Forshaw & Pasi, Hermes Explains
sources.abstract: A collection of short, accessible essays by thirty scholars answering common questions about 'Western esotericism' -- the broad academic label for astrology, alchemy, magic, and other hidden-knowledge traditions -- aimed at clearing up popular misconceptions about the field.

grail.definition: The Grail is the sacred vessel of Arthurian legend — the object of the great quest, identified with the cup of the Last Supper. It is defined less by what it is than by its being sought: unlike an ordinary *chalice*, the Grail is the lost and holy vessel whose pursuit is the whole point, glimpsed but rarely grasped.
grail.history: From Old French *graal*, 'cup, dish,' from Medieval Latin *gradalis*. Its sanctity was retro-fitted. In Chrétien de Troyes' *Perceval* the *graal* is a serving-dish carried in procession, not yet holy; only in the reworkings by clerical authors — above all the early-13th-century *Quest of the Holy Grail* — does it become the wonder-working chalice of the Last Supper, thick with theological meaning. The dinner-dish rose into the Holy Grail, and 'grail' now names any sought-after ultimate — the philosophers' stone as 'the Holy Grail of alchemy.'

sources.label: Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages
sources.abstract: A widely used introduction to what magic actually was in medieval Europe: who practiced it, what forms it took, and how it connected to religion, science, storytelling, and law. It also explains how fear of magic fed into the witch trials of later centuries.

poppet.definition: A poppet is a small effigy — a figure of clay, wax, or cloth made to stand for a person in sympathetic magic. What is done to the poppet is meant to befall the one it represents; it is the witch's doll, the image pierced, bound, or deformed to reach its target at a distance.
poppet.history: From Old French *poupette*, from Latin *puppa*, 'doll, girl' — the witch's effigy is literally a 'little doll,' and *puppet* is the very same word (while *poppet* survives as a term of endearment). The object is ancient: the Greek magical papyri and Roman curse deposits used figures with twisted heads and broken necks, and clay 'poppets' were buried alongside binding tablets, as at the Fountain of Anna Perenna in Rome. Those same deformities reappear later in northern European witchcraft, where the poppet becomes the classic instrument of harmful image-magic.

sources.label: Skinner, Magical Techniques and Implements in the Graeco-Egyptian Magical Papyri
sources.abstract: Skinner works through surviving magical papyrus scrolls from Roman-era Egypt to catalog the actual tools and step-by-step methods ancient magicians used, such as bowls and lamps for seeing visions, potions, and ritual objects, and traces how these methods were passed down into later European magic books.

sources.label: Lamont, In Blood and Ashes: Curse Tablets and Binding Spells in Ancient Greece
sources.abstract: Using newly published curse tablets alongside archaeological finds, Lamont shows that cursing rituals in ancient Greece were a common tool ordinary people -- tavern keepers, barbers, sailors -- used to settle everyday grudges and rivalries, not just a fringe practice of shady magicians.

reliquary.definition: A reliquary is a container made to hold holy relics — the bones or belongings of a saint — often a jeweled and precious box or shrine. Its purpose is enshrinement, not use: where a *censer* burns and a *chalice* offers, the reliquary keeps and displays sacred remains, honoring the power thought to reside in them.
reliquary.history: From Latin *reliquiae*, 'remains' (from *relinquere*, 'to leave behind') — the same root as *relic* and *relinquish*, a vessel for holy leftovers. Because the relics inside were held to carry the saint's power, the container grew correspondingly precious: Kieckhefer notes reliquaries set with gems to render honor to the saints thus enshrined, the power of stones linked with the power of the holy dead. Some were engineered so that oil poured in at the top ran out sanctified by proximity to the relic, and grimoires borrowed the image — carry the characters 'like a reliquary.'

sources.label: Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages
sources.abstract: A widely used introduction to what magic actually was in medieval Europe: who practiced it, what forms it took, and how it connected to religion, science, storytelling, and law. It also explains how fear of magic fed into the witch trials of later centuries.

sources.label: Page, Magic in the Cloister
sources.abstract: A study of English monks who, despite their religious vows, collected and studied magic texts as part of their intellectual curiosity. It shows that for these monks, magic and monastic religious life weren't necessarily opposites — they could fit together.

wand.definition: A wand is a rod that directs magical power — a slender staff or twig the magician holds to point, command, and channel. It contrasts with vessels and effigies: not a thing that contains or represents, but an instrument of the gesture itself, the extension of the caster's will along a line.
wand.history: From Old Norse *vöndr*, 'a supple rod, switch' (Proto-Germanic *wanduz*, from a root meaning 'to wind') — the magician's wand is, at bottom, a whippy twig you could bend; Latin's rival word *virga* gives us 'verge.' The instrument is as old as the literature: the wand is the standard accessory of the classical magician, first attested in the *Odyssey*, where Circe strikes her victims with her *rhabdos*. Ritual manuals kept it central — the operator's hazel wand among his swords and censer — down to the ceremonial magic of the grimoires and beyond.

sources.label: Page & Rider, The Routledge History of Medieval Magic
sources.abstract: A large reference collection that rounds up current scholarship on magic in medieval Europe (roughly 1100 to 1500), covering the texts people used, how the church and universities reacted, and how magic touched gender roles, art, medicine, and the early witch trials.

sources.label: Klaassen, The Transformations of Magic

Klaassen traces how interest in the first kind faded after 1500 while the second kept going and helped shape early modern magic.
